Courts consider several factors in deciding whether to extend time: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reason for the delay (whether it was inadvertent or deliberate); (3) whether the intended appellant has shown that the appeal has merit (i.e., a prima facie ground for the appeal); (4) prejudice to the respondent from granting the extension; and (5) whether refusing the extension would cause a substantial injustice. An extension is not automatic even for an administrative error — the court considers all factors in the interests of justice.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61.04(1); r.3.02
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Notice of Appeal — Time to Appeal and Extending Time

text:
Nakamura Electronics Inc. loses a commercial judgment for $800,000 at trial and intends to appeal to the Court of Appeal. Nakamura asks its counsel whether it must pay the judgment or whether it can stay execution pending appeal. Counsel must advise on the applicable test for obtaining a stay.

What is the test an appellant must satisfy to obtain a stay of execution of a money judgment pending appeal in Ontario?
opts: The filing of a notice of appeal automatically stays execution of the judgment pending the appeal; no motion is required.

To obtain a stay of execution pending appeal, the moving party must satisfy the three-part test from RJR-MacDonald: (1) there is a serious question to be tried (or a non-frivolous appeal); (2) the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted; and (3) the balance of convenience favours granting the stay.
A stay of execution pending appeal is available only for injunctions and equitable orders, not for money judgments.
To obtain a stay, the appellant need only demonstrate that the appeal is not frivolous; no other factors are considered.
expl: Under r.63.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the established test drawn from RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311, a party seeking a stay of execution of a judgment pending appeal must satisfy three criteria: (1) a serious question to be tried — whether the appeal raises a non-frivolous or arguable issue; (2) irreparable harm — the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted and must pay the judgment before the appeal is resolved; and (3) balance of convenience — which party will suffer greater harm from granting or refusing the stay. The filing of a notice of appeal alone does not automatically stay a money judgment; a motion for a stay is required. Courts weigh all three factors and have discretion to impose conditions, such as requiring security.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.63.02; RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Stay of Execution Pending Appeal — Test and Procedure

text:
A court order requires the defendant, Szymanski Technologies Inc., to deliver specific proprietary software source code to the plaintiff by a specified date. The date passes and Szymanski refuses to comply. The plaintiff seeks to enforce the order. The defendant argues that because the order relates to the delivery of property rather than the payment of money, no enforcement mechanism is available.

What enforcement mechanism is available to the plaintiff where a judgment debtor refuses to comply with a court order to deliver specific property?
opts: The defendant is correct — courts can only enforce money judgments; orders to deliver specific property cannot be enforced by any court process.

The plaintiff may bring a contempt motion against Szymanski for failing to comply with the court order; a party who disobeys a court order may be found in contempt and the court may impose sanctions including a fine, sequestration of assets, or imprisonment until the order is complied with.
The only remedy for non-compliance with an order to deliver specific property is to bring a new action for damages caused by the non-delivery.
The plaintiff may enforce the order by hiring a private enforcement agent to physically take the software source code from the defendant's premises without any further court process.
expl: Under r.60.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the inherent jurisdiction of the Superior Court, where a party fails to comply with a court order — including an order to deliver specific property — the judgment creditor may move for a finding of contempt of court. A party who disobeys a court order may be found in contempt and the court may impose sanctions including a fine, imprisonment, or sequestration of the party's assets until compliance is achieved. Contempt is a powerful enforcement tool for orders requiring specific performance of acts (non-money obligations) that cannot be enforced through a writ of seizure and sale or garnishment.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Contempt of Court — Enforcement of Court Orders

text:
Nakamura Media Inc. has obtained a judgment for $290,000 against Osei Broadcasting Corp., which has not paid. Nakamura's counsel believes Osei has assets but is unsure where they are. Counsel wants to examine an officer of Osei under oath to discover the extent and location of Osei's assets.

Under r.60.18 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what examination process is available to a judgment creditor who wishes to discover the assets of a judgment debtor?
opts: The judgment creditor must bring a motion and obtain a court order before any asset discovery examination can be conducted; the examination cannot proceed without prior judicial approval.

Under r.60.18, a judgment creditor may conduct an examination in aid of execution, which allows the judgment creditor to examine the judgment debtor (or, in the case of a corporation, an officer or director) under oath about the debtor's assets, income, and ability to pay the judgment; no prior court order is required to conduct this examination.
An examination in aid of execution is available only where the judgment debtor has already been found to be in contempt of a prior court order to pay.
The examination in aid of execution may be conducted only once per judgment; if the first examination reveals no assets, no further examination may be held.
expl: Under r.60.18 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a judgment creditor may examine a judgment debtor — or, where the debtor is a corporation, an officer, director, or employee with knowledge of the debtor's financial affairs — about the debtor's assets, property, debts, income, and ability to satisfy the judgment. This examination in aid of execution is conducted under oath and may be used by the judgment creditor to locate assets available for enforcement. No prior court order is required to schedule and conduct this examination; the judgment creditor issues a notice of examination. The information obtained can then be used to identify assets for garnishment, seizure, or other enforcement measures.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.60.18
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 14 — Enforcement of Judgments | Examination in Aid of Execution — r.60.18

text:
A class proceeding involving a data breach has been certified. Class counsel proposes to give notice to class members by posting a notice on the defendant's website and by sending an email to all class members whose email addresses are known. The defendant objects that the notice plan is insufficient, while two class members object that the email notice is too burdensome and they prefer mail notification.

Under the Class Proceedings Act, 1992, who has the authority to approve the method and content of notice to class members, and what is the standard applied?
opts: Class counsel has sole discretion over the method and content of notice; the court plays no role in approving the notice plan.
The defendant has the right to veto any notice plan it considers insufficient; the defendant's consent to the notice plan is required before any notice may be given.

The court must approve the method and content of notice to class members; the notice must be given in the best practicable manner in the circumstances, and the court determines whether the proposed plan satisfies this standard.
Individual class members must consent to their chosen method of receiving notice; if any class member objects to the proposed notice method, that member is automatically excluded from the class.
expl: Under the Class Proceedings Act, 1992 (S.O. 1992, c. 6) and the Rules of Civil Procedure, the court must approve both the method and the content of notice to class members. The standard is that notice must be given in the best practicable manner in the circumstances, taking into account the number of class members, the cost of notice, and the most effective means of reaching class members. The court determines whether the proposed notice plan — including the channels of communication, the timing, and the content — satisfies this standard. Neither class counsel nor the defendant has unilateral authority to determine the notice plan; court approval is required. Individual class members who prefer a different method of notice do not have a veto over the approved notice plan.
caseRef: Class Proceedings Act, 1992, S.O. 1992, c. 6, s.17; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.12.07
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Class Proceedings | Notice to Class Members — Court Approval Required

text:
The Crown proceeds summarily against the accused for assault (a hybrid offence). The offence allegedly occurred on January 15. Defence counsel discovers the Crown laid the information on August 10 of the same year — seven and a half months later. Defence brings an application to dismiss the information as out of time.

Under the Criminal Code, what is the limitation period within which an information charging a summary conviction offence must be laid?
opts: There is no limitation period for summary conviction offences; charges may be laid at any time.
The information must be laid within 12 months of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
The information must be laid within 6 months of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
The information must be laid within 2 years of the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
expl: Under s. 786(2) of the Criminal Code, no proceedings shall be instituted for a summary conviction offence more than six months after the time when the subject-matter of the proceedings arose, unless the prosecutor and the defendant consent to proceedings being instituted after that time. The six-month limitation period is one of the defining features distinguishing summary conviction proceedings from indictable proceedings, which have no limitation period. On these facts, the information was laid approximately seven and a half months after the alleged offence — beyond the six-month window — and the application to dismiss should succeed absent consent from both parties.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 786(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Summary Conviction Procedure — Limitation Period

text:
Anson is charged with conspiracy to commit murder. His lawyer advises him that conspiracy to commit murder is listed in s. 469 of the Criminal Code and that his mode of trial options are limited. Anson wishes to be tried by a superior court judge alone without a jury.

Under the Criminal Code, may Anson be tried by a superior court judge alone for an s. 469 offence, and if so, what is required?
opts: No — all s. 469 offences must be tried by a superior court judge and jury; there is no mechanism to remove the jury.
Yes — Anson may unilaterally elect to be tried by judge alone by notifying the registrar of his preference before the trial commences.
Yes — under s. 473 of the Criminal Code, a judge-alone trial for an s. 469 offence may be ordered if both the accused and the Attorney General consent in writing and the court approves.
No — Anson must first complete a preliminary inquiry before any application for judge-alone trial may be made.
expl: Under s. 473 of the Criminal Code, a superior court judge may order that a trial for an s. 469 offence proceed without a jury if both the accused and the Attorney General (or their counsel) consent in writing and the judge is satisfied it is in the interests of justice to do so. The default mode of trial for s. 469 offences is a superior court judge and jury — unlike the standard s. 536 election offences where the accused can unilaterally choose judge alone. For s. 469 offences, the jury can only be removed by the dual-consent process under s. 473.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 469, s. 473
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Absolute and Exclusive Jurisdiction — s. 469 and s. 473

text:
Marco initially elected to be tried by a superior court judge and jury. He has been committed to stand trial following a preliminary inquiry. He now wishes to change his election to trial by provincial court judge alone. The Crown has not consented.

Under s. 561 of the Criminal Code, may Marco re-elect to trial by provincial court judge alone after committal, and does he need Crown consent?
opts: No — once an accused is committed to stand trial after a preliminary inquiry, no re-election is possible.
Yes — Marco may re-elect downward to provincial court judge alone before trial commences, without Crown consent, within the timelines prescribed in s. 561.
Yes — Marco may re-elect downward at any time before trial, but he requires Crown consent regardless of when the re-election is made.
Yes — Marco may re-elect but only with leave of a superior court judge.
expl: Section 561 of the Criminal Code governs re-elections after committal for trial. Under s. 561(1)(a), an accused who has been committed to stand trial may re-elect the mode of trial at any time before the trial commences. Where the accused is re-electing to a lower mode — from superior court judge and jury to provincial court judge alone — Crown consent is not required provided the re-election is made within the time limits specified in s. 561. The mechanism allows a 'downward' re-election without Crown consent, though the accused must provide notice in the prescribed form.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 561(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Court Structure and Jurisdiction | Re-Election of Mode of Trial — s. 561

text:
Ahmed is charged with criminal harassment of his former partner. As a condition of his bail release, the justice imposes a condition that Ahmed have no contact with the complainant, not attend within 500 metres of her residence, workplace, or school, and surrender his passport. Ahmed's lawyer argues the no-contact condition is excessive because Ahmed and the complainant share custody of their child.

What principle governs the court's power to impose bail conditions, and how should the no-contact condition be assessed in light of the shared custody issue?
opts: Bail conditions must be the most restrictive measures possible to protect the public and the complainant.

Bail conditions must be reasonable and proportionate to the ground of detention they address; the court has discretion to carve out exceptions (such as child access exchanges) to prevent conditions from being unduly harsh.
A no-contact condition with an alleged victim is automatically required in any domestic violence or harassment case by operation of law.
Bail conditions are controlled exclusively by the Crown; the court must impose whatever the Crown requests.
expl: Under the Criminal Code, bail conditions must be reasonable and tailored to address the specific ground of detention. The ladder principle requires the least restrictive form of release sufficient to address the risk. A no-contact condition is commonly imposed in harassment cases to protect the complainant (secondary ground) and is generally appropriate. However, where the accused and complainant share parenting obligations, courts routinely carve out exceptions to allow for child access — for example, contact facilitated through a third party or during scheduled access exchanges — to avoid making the condition unduly harsh. The court must balance victim protection with the practical realities of the parties' ongoing obligations.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Conditions — Reasonableness and Proportionality

text:
Yolanda was released on bail six months ago. She has since obtained stable employment, moved to a new residence with her parents as supervisors, and has attended all court appearances without fail. She applies under s. 520 to review her bail conditions, which she finds overly restrictive. The Crown argues there has been no 'change in circumstances' and the bail review should be dismissed.

Must an accused demonstrate a 'change in circumstances' since the original bail hearing to succeed on a s. 520 bail review application?
opts: Yes — the accused must show a material change in circumstances since the original bail order before the reviewing judge may vary the conditions.
No — the reviewing judge exercises a completely fresh and independent discretion; no change in circumstances is required.
No — the accused need only demonstrate that the original bail conditions were unreasonable when imposed.
Yes — but the accused must also demonstrate that the original justice made an error of law.
expl: Under s. 520 of the Criminal Code, a bail review is not a free-standing fresh hearing with no preconditions. Canadian courts have held that, to succeed on a bail review, the accused must demonstrate either that the original hearing was conducted on an error of law or principle, or that there has been a material change in circumstances since the original order was made. Yolanda's improved circumstances — employment, stable residence, compliance record — may constitute a material change in circumstances that justifies revisiting the bail conditions. Without such a change or a legal error below, the review judge should not simply substitute their own view.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 520
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Review — Changed Circumstances

text:
Keon is released on bail with his aunt, Marlene, as his surety. The bail order requires Keon to reside at Marlene's home, observe a 10:00 p.m. curfew, and report to police weekly. Marlene pledges $5,000 as her recognizance. Three months into his release, Keon starts coming home well past midnight on multiple occasions. Marlene confronts him but he continues to breach the curfew.

What is the proper course of action for Marlene if she can no longer control or supervise Keon?
opts: Marlene must immediately report Keon's breach to police and will automatically lose her $5,000 pledge.

Marlene may notify the court that she is no longer able to supervise Keon, and may be relieved of her obligations as surety; the court may then revoke the recognizance and issue an arrest warrant for Keon.
Marlene must continue as surety for the entire period until trial; she cannot withdraw once appointed.
Marlene's only remedy is to apply for a variation of bail conditions to reduce Keon's curfew obligation.
expl: Under the Criminal Code, a surety who can no longer supervise an accused may surrender the accused into custody and apply to be relieved of their obligations. Section 767.1 provides a mechanism for a surety to bring the accused before a justice and be discharged from the recognizance. Once the surety surrenders the accused, the court may revoke the bail and issue a warrant for arrest if the accused is not surrendered directly. Estreatment of the surety's pledge occurs only if the accused breaches bail conditions and the court proceedings are not initiated to recover the surety — not automatically upon every breach report.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 767, s. 767.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Surety — Role and Estreatment

text:
During a robbery investigation, police collected witness statements, video footage, and forensic reports. Crown counsel reviews the file and discloses what is in the Crown brief but does not review the complete police investigation file. Some relevant witness statements in the police file are never disclosed to defence. Defence argues that the Crown's duty extends to these statements.

Under the Stinchcombe duty to disclose, does the Crown's disclosure obligation extend to materials in the police investigation file that were not placed in the Crown brief?
opts: No — the Crown's duty is limited to materials in the Crown brief that the prosecutor personally reviewed.
No — the police and the Crown are separate entities; the Crown cannot be responsible for materials it never received.

Yes — the Crown is deemed to be in constructive possession of all materials gathered by the police in the investigation; the duty to disclose extends to all relevant material in the possession of police acting in a prosecutorial capacity.
Yes — but only if the accused can prove the police deliberately withheld the material from the Crown.
expl: Stinchcombe established that the Crown's duty to disclose extends beyond materials physically in the prosecutor's file. The Crown is deemed to have constructive possession of all materials in the possession of the police who conducted the investigation because the police act as agents of the prosecutorial process. Accordingly, the Crown must inquire into and disclose relevant materials held by police even if those materials were never placed in the formal Crown brief. The failure to review and disclose relevant police investigation materials is a breach of the Stinchcombe obligation.
caseRef: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Constructive Possession — Police Investigation Files

text:
At a robbery trial, defence counsel requests the complete personnel file of the investigating detective, including disciplinary records, performance reviews, and sick leave records from the past five years. Crown refuses, arguing the material is irrelevant and that Stinchcombe does not require disclosure of a police officer's entire employment history.

Under the Stinchcombe disclosure framework, what is the relevance threshold that determines whether material must be disclosed to the defence?
opts: The Crown must disclose any material that might reasonably be used by the accused in making full answer and defence, which includes material that is potentially relevant.
The Crown must disclose only material it considers directly admissible at trial.
The Crown must disclose all material regardless of relevance; the defence determines what is useful.
The Crown must disclose material only if the accused can demonstrate on a balance of probabilities that it will assist the defence.
expl: Stinchcombe established a broad relevance standard for disclosure: the Crown must disclose information that is not clearly irrelevant or clearly privileged. More specifically, any information that may reasonably be used by the accused in making full answer and defence must be disclosed. This includes information that is potentially useful to the defence even if not directly admissible. For police officer personnel files, R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3 established a specific framework: the Crown must disclose records of serious misconduct by a police witness if those records are relevant to issues at trial (including credibility). However, a blanket request for all employment records without demonstrated potential relevance may be refused. The relevance threshold is broad but not unlimited.
caseRef: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; R. v. McNeil, 2009 SCC 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Stinchcombe — Relevance Threshold

text:
Oliver is charged with break and enter. At his first appearance, the Crown provides him with a one-page synopsis rather than full disclosure. The Crown explains that the full disclosure package will be ready in three weeks, by which time Oliver will already have been required to make his election. Oliver's lawyer objects that the election should be postponed until full disclosure is provided.

Under the Stinchcombe duty to disclose, is defence counsel's objection legally sound?
opts: No — the election must proceed on the scheduled date regardless of whether full disclosure has been provided; disclosure is a separate process.

Yes — Stinchcombe requires that initial disclosure be made before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial; requiring election before disclosure violates the right to make full answer and defence.
No — a synopsis is sufficient disclosure for the purpose of election; full production may follow later.
Yes — but only if the charge is a serious indictable offence; for minor offences the election may proceed without disclosure.
expl: In R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326, the Supreme Court held that the duty to disclose must be complied with before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial or enter a plea. The rationale is that the accused cannot meaningfully decide whether to elect a jury trial, a judge-alone trial, or a preliminary inquiry — and cannot make a fully informed guilty plea — without knowing what evidence the Crown holds. Providing only a synopsis and requiring election before full disclosure violates the Stinchcombe obligation. The appropriate remedy would be an adjournment of the election until initial disclosure is complete.
caseRef: R. v. Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Timing of Disclosure — Before Election and Plea

text:
Ivan is charged with sexual assault. He applies under ss. 278.3–278.5 of the Criminal Code for production of the complainant's counselling records held by a social worker. At the first stage, the trial judge determines whether to order the records produced to the court. Ivan argues the records likely contain relevant information about the complainant's mental state at the time of the alleged assault.

Under ss. 278.4–278.5 of the Criminal Code (Mills regime), what must Ivan establish at Stage One of the two-stage production process?
opts: Ivan must prove, on a balance of probabilities, that the records contain information relevant to his guilt or innocence.
Ivan must satisfy the judge that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness, and that production is necessary in the interests of justice.
Ivan must establish that the complainant consented to disclosure of the records to the court.
Ivan must prove that the records are available nowhere else and that the defence cannot obtain the same information through cross-examination.
expl: Under s. 278.5(1) of the Criminal Code, at Stage One of the Mills regime, the judge shall order production of the record to the judge for review if satisfied that: (a) the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness; and (b) production of the record is necessary in the interests of justice. The 'likely relevant' standard is less stringent than balance of probabilities — it means there is a reasonable possibility the records contain relevant information. The accused cannot rely on assertions that fall within s. 278.3(4) (the enumerated impermissible bases, e.g., that the complainant has a history of making complaints).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.5(1); R. v. Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Third Party Records — Mills Regime Stage One

text:
Conrad is charged with sexual assault. In his s. 278.3 application, he argues that the complainant's therapy records should be produced because: (i) the complainant has a history of sexual activity with third parties; (ii) the complainant previously made a complaint of sexual assault against another person; and (iii) the therapy records may contain information about the complainant's perception and memory at the relevant time.

Under s. 278.3(4) of the Criminal Code, which of Conrad's stated grounds is an impermissible basis for the application?
opts: Grounds (i) and (ii) are impermissible bases; ground (iii) is a permissible basis.
Ground (iii) is the only impermissible basis; grounds (i) and (ii) may support a production application.
All three grounds are impermissible under s. 278.3(4).
Ground (i) is permissible if the prior sexual activity involved the accused; grounds (ii) and (iii) are impermissible.
expl: Section 278.3(4) of the Criminal Code lists specific assertions that are deemed insufficient, alone, to establish that a record is likely relevant. These include assertions that: (a) the record exists; (b) the record relates to medical or psychiatric treatment; (c) the record may reveal a pattern of conduct; (d) the record relates to the credibility of the complainant; (e) the accused needs the records to cross-examine the complainant; (f) the records may contain an admission; (g) the complainant has a history of making complaints; and (h) the complainant has previously been sexually active with the accused or others. Grounds (i) (prior sexual activity with third parties) and (ii) (history of making sexual assault complaints) are both expressly listed as impermissible bases under s. 278.3(4). Ground (iii) — that the records may contain information about the complainant's perception and memory at the relevant time — relates to a specific testimonial issue (the witness's perception and memory capacity) and is not listed as an impermissible basis.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.3(4)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Third Party Records — Impermissible Bases

text:
At a preliminary inquiry for aggravated assault, the Crown's sole witness recants her identification of the accused and states she cannot now say who attacked her. No other identification evidence is adduced. Defence moves for a discharge. The preliminary inquiry judge finds there is no evidence capable of supporting a conviction on identification.

What order should the preliminary inquiry judge make, and what is the effect of that order?
opts: Commit the accused to stand trial because the recantation goes to credibility, not to the legal sufficiency of the evidence.
Discharge the accused; the discharge is equivalent to an acquittal and the Crown cannot re-lay the charge.
Discharge the accused under s. 548(1)(b); a discharge at the preliminary inquiry stage is not an acquittal and the Crown may re-lay the charge by direct indictment.
Refer the matter to the superior court for a determination of whether the evidence is sufficient.
expl: Under s. 548(1) of the Criminal Code, where the evidence adduced is not sufficient to put the accused on trial, the justice shall order the accused discharged. On the facts, with the sole identification witness having recanted and no other identification evidence, there is no evidence on which a properly instructed jury could convict — the committal standard is not met and discharge is mandatory. However, a discharge at the preliminary inquiry is not an acquittal for the purpose of double jeopardy. The Crown retains the ability to lay a fresh charge or to proceed by way of direct indictment (s. 577) to bring the accused to trial without a further preliminary inquiry, if additional evidence emerges or the Crown decides to exercise that discretion.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 548(1)(b), s. 577; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(h)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Preliminary Inquiry — Discharge and Its Effect

text:
Carla is charged with mischief under $5,000 — a hybrid offence. The Crown elects to proceed summarily. Carla's lawyer advises her that the Crown's election changes what mode of trial options are available to Carla.

When the Crown elects to proceed summarily on a hybrid offence, does the accused retain the right to elect their mode of trial?
opts: Yes — the accused always retains the right to elect the mode of trial regardless of the Crown's election.
No — where the Crown elects to proceed summarily, the matter is tried in the provincial court as a summary conviction proceeding; the accused has no right to elect their mode of trial.
Yes — the accused may elect to be tried by a superior court judge even where the Crown has elected summarily.
No — but the accused may appeal the Crown's election to the superior court for review.
expl: For hybrid offences, the Crown's election to proceed summarily converts the matter into a summary conviction proceeding for all purposes. Summary conviction proceedings are conducted before a provincial court judge without jury, and the accused has no right to elect a mode of trial. The accused's right to elect under s. 536 of the Criminal Code applies only to indictable offences (that are not within the absolute or exclusive jurisdiction of either the provincial or superior court). When the Crown proceeds summarily, the accused is not entitled to elect a superior court or jury trial.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 536(2); interpretation of hybrid offences
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Hybrid Offences — Crown Election Effect

text:
Lia is charged with robbery. She wishes to plead not guilty to robbery but guilty to theft, which she argues is an included offence arising from the same transaction. The Crown consents to this plea arrangement. The trial judge must determine whether to accept the plea to theft.

Assuming the Crown consents under s. 606(4), what additional condition must the court be satisfied of before accepting Lia's guilty plea to theft?
opts: The court must be satisfied that theft is a lesser included offence of robbery as a matter of law.
The court need only confirm the Crown's consent; no further inquiry is required once consent is established.

The court must be satisfied of the same s. 606(1.1) conditions for any guilty plea — that the plea is voluntary, informed, and that the facts admitted are sufficient to support a conviction for theft.
The court must hold a preliminary inquiry to determine whether the facts support the lesser charge before accepting the plea.
expl: Under s. 606(4) of the Criminal Code, where the Crown consents, the court may accept a plea of guilty to a lesser or included offence. However, Crown consent is a necessary but not sufficient condition. Before accepting any guilty plea — including a plea to a lesser or included offence — the court must still conduct the s. 606(1.1) inquiry to be satisfied that: (a) the accused is pleading voluntarily; (b) the accused understands the nature of the charge to which they are pleading (here, theft); (c) the accused understands the consequences of the plea; and (d) the facts admitted by the accused are sufficient to support a conviction for the offence (theft). The s. 606(1.1) safeguards apply to all guilty pleas regardless of the mechanism under which they arise.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Guilty Plea to Lesser Included Offence — s. 606

text:
The accused pleads guilty to assault on the basis of a joint submission for a conditional sentence by the Crown and defence. The sentencing judge states: 'I find this proposed sentence far too lenient given the violence involved. I intend to impose a nine-month custodial sentence instead.' Defence counsel argues the judge must accept the joint submission.

Under R. v. Anthony-Cook, 2016 SCC 43, what is the standard a sentencing judge must apply before departing from a joint submission on sentence?
opts: The judge may reject a joint submission whenever the judge disagrees with it on the merits.
The judge must accept a joint submission unless the proposed sentence would bring the administration of justice into disrepute or is otherwise contrary to the public interest.
The judge may reject a joint submission if the proposed sentence falls outside the normal range for similar offences.
The judge must accept a joint submission in all circumstances; joint submissions are binding on the court.
expl: In R. v. Anthony-Cook, 2016 SCC 43, the Supreme Court of Canada held that a sentencing judge should not depart from a joint submission on sentence unless the proposed sentence would bring the administration of justice into disrepute or is otherwise contrary to the public interest. This is a high threshold — higher than 'unfit' or 'outside the normal range.' The rationale is that joint submissions reflect a complex negotiation involving give and take by both parties, and certainty in their outcome is critical to the integrity of the plea bargaining process. The judge may reject the submission only where a reasonable and informed person would view the sentence as fundamentally unacceptable, not merely where the judge would impose a different sentence.
caseRef: R. v. Anthony-Cook, 2016 SCC 43
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Plea Bargaining — Joint Submissions

text:
At a preliminary inquiry for robbery, the Crown seeks to rely on a written statement from a witness who is unable to attend. Crown counsel argues the statement is sufficient evidence for the purpose of committal. Defence objects that hearsay evidence cannot support committal.

Under the Criminal Code, may hearsay evidence be admitted and relied upon at a preliminary inquiry to support committal?
opts: No — the rules of evidence at preliminary inquiries are identical to trial; hearsay evidence is not admissible.

Yes — s. 540 of the Criminal Code specifically provides that hearsay evidence may be received at a preliminary inquiry if the justice considers it appropriate, even if that evidence would be inadmissible at trial.
Yes — hearsay evidence is automatically admissible at all preliminary inquiries without restriction.
No — a preliminary inquiry justice has no authority to admit evidence that would be inadmissible at trial.
expl: Section 540(7) of the Criminal Code expressly provides that the justice at a preliminary inquiry may receive as evidence any information that would otherwise be admissible as evidence or any other information, if the justice is of the opinion that such other information is reliable. This provision allows the justice to receive evidence — including hearsay — that would not be admissible at trial, provided the justice considers it reliable. The purpose of the preliminary inquiry is to determine whether there is sufficient evidence for committal, not to conduct a full trial on admissibility rules. This is one of the key distinctions between the preliminary inquiry and the trial.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 540(7)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5: Pre-Trial Procedure | Preliminary Inquiry — Admissible Evidence

text:
At a robbery trial, the Crown calls the alleged eyewitness. The witness now claims he cannot remember seeing anything and gives evidence that contradicts an earlier written statement he gave police. The Crown applies under s. 9(1) of the Canada Evidence Act to have the witness declared adverse.

Under s. 9(1) of the Canada Evidence Act, if the court declares the witness adverse, what is the immediate procedural consequence for the Crown?
opts: The Crown may immediately tender the witness's prior statement as substantive evidence of the truth of its contents.
The Crown may cross-examine the witness in the same manner as an opposing party would cross-examine a hostile witness, including asking leading questions and challenging the witness's credibility.
The court must declare a mistrial if a Crown witness is declared adverse.
The Crown may replace the witness with a different witness who will give the same evidence.
expl: Under s. 9(1) of the Canada Evidence Act, where a party's own witness gives evidence that is adverse to that party, the court may, upon application, declare the witness adverse. Once a witness is declared adverse, the party calling the witness is permitted to cross-examine the witness as if they were an opposing party's witness — including asking leading questions and challenging the witness on inconsistencies. The declaration of adversity allows the calling party to treat the witness as a hostile witness for the purpose of credibility attack. Note that the prior statement (under s. 9(1)) can be used to impeach but may or may not be substantive evidence depending on whether the K.G.B. criteria are met.
caseRef: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 9(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Adverse Witness — s. 9 Canada Evidence Act

text:
Ivan is charged with robbery, an offence that carries a maximum penalty of life imprisonment. His trial will be by judge and jury. His lawyer is advising him about the jury selection process, specifically regarding any right to challenge prospective jurors without cause.

Following the 2019 Criminal Code amendments to jury selection, does an accused charged with robbery have the right to peremptory challenges, and if so, how many?
opts: Yes — an accused charged with an offence carrying a maximum sentence of life imprisonment has 20 peremptory challenges.
Yes — the accused has 12 peremptory challenges for an offence carrying life imprisonment under s. 634(2)(a).
No — peremptory challenges were abolished by the Criminal Code amendments that came into force on September 19, 2019.
Yes — the accused and Crown each have an equal number of peremptory challenges determined by the trial judge.
expl: The Criminal Code and Other Acts (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act, S.C. 2019, c. 25 (Bill C-75) abolished peremptory challenges effective September 19, 2019. Prior to this amendment, s. 634 of the Criminal Code provided peremptory challenges (the right to reject a juror without giving reasons). After the 2019 amendments, s. 634 no longer provides for peremptory challenges. Accused persons (and the Crown) now rely exclusively on challenges for cause under s. 638 (which require demonstrated grounds) and the 'stand aside' mechanism that replaced peremptory challenges to some degree. Ivan's trial post-2019 will have no peremptory challenges available.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 634 (repealed by S.C. 2019, c. 25)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Jury Selection — Peremptory Challenges

text:
Ahmed is an accused Muslim man charged with a terrorism-related offence that received extensive pre-trial media coverage. Defence counsel seeks to challenge potential jurors for cause, arguing that some jurors may hold prejudice based on Ahmed's religion or background that would prevent them from deciding the case impartially.

Under s. 638 of the Criminal Code, on what ground may defence counsel challenge a potential juror for cause in this scenario?
opts: A challenge for cause may be brought only if the prospective juror has been convicted of a criminal offence.
A challenge for cause may be brought on the ground that the prospective juror is not impartial as between the Crown and the accused.
A challenge for cause is not available in terrorism cases because the safety of the public requires impartial jurors to be retained.
A challenge for cause may only be brought where the accused demonstrates a specific prior interaction between the juror and the accused.
expl: Section 638(1)(b) of the Criminal Code provides that a juror may be challenged for cause on the ground that the juror is not indifferent between the Crown and the accused. This provision encompasses the situation where racial, religious, or other forms of bias — including bias inflamed by pre-trial publicity or the nature of the charge — may prevent a juror from impartially weighing the evidence. The Supreme Court in R. v. Williams, [1998] 1 SCR 1128 recognized that widespread racial prejudice may constitute a proper basis for challenging jurors for cause. Defence counsel may ask the court for permission to challenge potential jurors for cause on the ground of partiality arising from religious or racial bias.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 638(1)(b); R. v. Williams, [1998] 1 SCR 1128
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Jury Selection — Challenge for Cause

text:
In a judge-alone trial for fraud, the Crown has closed its case. The accused's counsel elected not to call any defence evidence. The trial judge must now direct the parties on the order in which they will deliver their closing submissions.

Under the Criminal Code, in what order do the parties deliver their closing addresses where the accused did not call evidence?
opts: The accused addresses first, then the Crown addresses last.
The Crown addresses first, then the accused addresses last.
The Crown addresses first, the accused responds, and then the Crown has the right to reply.
The order of addresses is entirely at the trial judge's discretion.
expl: Under s. 651(3) of the Criminal Code, where the accused did not call evidence after the close of the Crown's case, the accused has the right to address the jury or judge last — after the Crown's address. The general rule under s. 651 is that where the accused calls no evidence, the Crown speaks first and the accused speaks second (and last). This gives the accused — who has the burden of nothing to prove — the advantage of the last word where they did not adduce evidence. Where the accused does call evidence, the order reverses: the accused addresses first and the Crown last (with a possible reply).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 651(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6: Trial — Procedure and Evidence | Order of Addresses — s. 651

text:
In a fraud trial, the Crown seeks to admit records of a bank's daily transaction logs without calling each bank employee who created the entries. Crown argues these are admissible as business records under s. 30 of the Canada Evidence Act. Defence objects that the original authors of the entries must testify.

Under s. 30 of the Canada Evidence Act, which of the following conditions must be established for the transaction logs to be admitted as business records?
opts: The Crown must call the employee who made each individual entry to authenticate the record.
The records must be produced from the originator's personal computer only and must not be electronically transmitted copies.

The record must have been made in the usual and ordinary course of business, and the record must have been made at or near the time of the act, transaction, occurrence, or event it purports to record.
The records are admissible only if the original document is produced; copies are not admissible under s. 30.
expl: Section 30 of the Canada Evidence Act creates an exception to the hearsay rule for business records. Under s. 30(1), a record made in the usual and ordinary course of business is admissible as evidence of the facts stated therein, provided: (a) the record was made in the usual and ordinary course of business; (b) it was the usual and ordinary course of that business to make such records; and (c) the record was made at or near the time of the act, transaction, occurrence, or event it purports to record. The calling of each individual employee who made an entry is not required — that is the very purpose of the business records exception. A party may produce a certificate of authenticity under s. 30(7) in lieu of the author's testimony.
caseRef: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 30(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Business Records Exception — s. 30 Canada Evidence Act

text:
The Crown seeks to admit a statement that the complainant made to a police officer two days after the alleged sexual assault. The statement was recorded on video. The complainant is alive and available to testify but refuses to cooperate. The trial judge must assess threshold reliability.

Under R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57, which of the following BEST describes how the trial judge should assess threshold reliability?
opts: By assessing whether the statement, if true, would be sufficient to prove the offence beyond a reasonable doubt.
By weighing the ultimate reliability of the statement on the balance of probabilities.

By examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement to determine whether there are adequate substitutes for the oath, presence, and cross-examination that the trier of fact would normally have.
By considering only whether the statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation.
expl: In R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57, the Supreme Court clarified that threshold reliability is assessed by examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement — what the court calls 'circumstantial guarantees of reliability.' The question is whether those circumstances provide adequate substitutes for the three traditional safeguards of in-court testimony: the oath, the presence of the trier of fact (who observes demeanour), and cross-examination. Alternatively, the statement may be admitted based on procedural reliability if the accused has the opportunity to test the evidence. The judge does not assess ultimate reliability (that is for the trier of fact) or require proof beyond a reasonable doubt.
caseRef: R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57; R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Hearsay — Threshold Reliability (Khelawon)

text:
The Crown's key witness testifies about the robbery she witnessed. In cross-examination, defence counsel suggests the witness fabricated her account only after speaking to investigators. Crown counsel then seeks to introduce a statement the witness made to a friend the morning after the robbery — before she ever spoke to police — in which she described the accused as the perpetrator.

What is the general rule regarding prior consistent statements, and does an exception apply in this scenario?
opts: Prior consistent statements are admissible as of right because they corroborate the witness's in-court testimony.

Prior consistent statements are generally inadmissible (self-serving hearsay), but an exception applies where the defence has alleged recent fabrication; the prior consistent statement may be admitted to rebut the fabrication allegation, provided it pre-dates the alleged motive to fabricate.
Prior consistent statements are admissible because they were made to a private individual rather than to police.
Prior consistent statements are inadmissible in all circumstances; the witness's credibility must be assessed only on in-court testimony.
expl: The general rule in Canadian evidence law is that a party cannot introduce their own witness's prior statements consistent with the witness's in-court testimony — these are self-serving and inadmissible hearsay. However, one established exception applies where the opposing party has alleged recent fabrication — that is, alleged that the witness invented the story at some point after the alleged event, usually in response to a motive that arose after the event. Where such an allegation is made, the party calling the witness may introduce a prior consistent statement made before the alleged motive to fabricate arose. The purpose of the exception is to rebut the fabrication allegation, not to confirm the truth of the statement. On these facts, defence alleged the witness fabricated her account after speaking to investigators; the prior statement to her friend pre-dates that contact and falls within the exception.
caseRef: R. v. Stirling, 2008 SCC 10; R. v. O'Brien, [1978] 1 SCR 591 (recent fabrication exception)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Consistent Statements — Recent Fabrication Exception

text:
At trial for robbery, the eyewitness testifies that she identified the accused in a police photo lineup conducted two days after the robbery. She picks out the accused's photo and says it is the person who robbed her. Crown seeks to have the officer who conducted the lineup testify that the witness identified the same photo.

Is evidence of the witness's prior photo lineup identification admissible, and if so, on what basis?
opts: No — prior identification evidence is a prior consistent statement and is inadmissible on that basis.
Yes — prior identification is a recognized exception to the rule against prior consistent statements; the identifying officer may testify about the prior identification made by the witness.
Yes — but only if the identification occurred within 24 hours of the crime; later identifications are excluded as prior consistent statements.
No — the only admissible identification evidence is the witness's in-court identification of the accused.
expl: Prior identification evidence — a witness's previous act of identifying the accused — is a well-recognized exception to the general rule against prior consistent statements. Prior identification is admissible both through the identifying witness and through officers or others who witnessed the identification procedure. The rationale is that an identification made closer to the time of the offence when memory is fresher is often more reliable than an in-court identification made months or years later. The identifying officer may testify about the lineup and what the witness did, and the witness may confirm the prior identification. The evidence is admissible for the truth of the identification.
caseRef: R. v. Tat (1997), 117 CCC (3d) 481 (Ont. CA); R. v. Baltovich, 2004 CanLII 34404 (Ont. CA)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Identification — Exception to Prior Consistent Statement Rule

text:
The accused is charged with sexual assault. He wishes to introduce evidence at trial that the complainant had consensual sexual intercourse with him on a prior occasion. He argues this prior sexual activity is relevant to whether the complainant consented on the occasion charged.

Under s. 276 of the Criminal Code, which of the following is the correct procedure for admitting this evidence?
opts: The evidence is automatically admissible because prior consent is directly relevant to the issue of consent on the occasion charged.
The evidence is automatically inadmissible under s. 276; prior sexual activity can never be adduced in a sexual assault trial.
The accused must bring a s. 276 application before the trial judge; the judge holds a voir dire and may admit the evidence only if it meets the specific criteria in s. 276(2).
The accused may adduce the prior sexual activity evidence as long as he gives notice to the Crown at least 7 days before trial.
expl: Section 276 of the Criminal Code creates a two-step process for prior sexual activity evidence. Section 276(1) prohibits admission of such evidence to support twin myths: (a) that the complainant is more likely to have consented, or (b) that she is less credible. Section 276(2) provides that prior sexual activity evidence is admissible only if: (a) it relates to a specific instance of sexual activity; (b) it is relevant to an issue at trial; (c) it has significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice; and (d) it does not engage the twin myths. The procedure under s. 276.1 requires the accused to bring a written application, serve it on the Crown and complainant's counsel, and the judge holds a voir dire before ruling on admissibility.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276, s. 276.1, s. 276.2; R. v. Seaboyer, [1991] 2 SCR 577
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7: Evidence | Prior Sexual Activity Evidence — s. 276

text:
The accused was driving a boat and hit a submerged log. The engine failed. A passenger was suffering a severe allergic reaction and would die without immediate medical attention. The nearest dock was on private property and the owner had previously refused access to the accused. The accused docked on the private property without permission, committing trespass, in order to save the passenger's life.

Under R. v. Perka, [1984] 2 SCR 232, which of the following correctly describes the three elements of the necessity defence?
opts: An imminent peril or danger; no reasonable legal alternative; the harm avoided was proportionate to the harm caused.
A situation of clear and present danger; no prior fault by the accused; the accused acted instinctively.
The accused faced a moral dilemma; the harm caused was the lesser evil; the accused obtained judicial approval before acting.
An emergency beyond the accused's control; the accused consulted with others before acting; the harm avoided substantially outweighed the harm caused.
expl: In R. v. Perka, [1984] 2 SCR 232, the Supreme Court of Canada recognized necessity as an excuse defence (not a justification) with three requirements: (1) imminent peril or danger — the threat of harm must be imminent, not merely anticipated; (2) no reasonable legal alternative — the accused must have had no lawful way out; and (3) proportionality — the harm inflicted must be proportionate to the harm avoided. All three must be established. On these facts: (1) imminent danger exists (dying passenger); (2) no legal alternative (private property is the only option; public dock is out of reach); (3) trespass is proportionate to saving a life. The defence is available.
caseRef: R. v. Perka, [1984] 2 SCR 232; R. v. Latimer, 2001 SCC 1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8: Criminal Defences | Necessity Defence — Perka Elements

text:
Hugo is convicted of two counts of robbery on the same occasion — he robbed two different individuals at the same time and place. The sentencing judge must decide whether to impose consecutive or concurrent sentences.

Which principle guides whether sentences for multiple offences should be served consecutively or concurrently?
opts: Sentences for offences arising from the same transaction must always be concurrent; consecutive sentences require the offences to arise from entirely separate events on different days.
All sentences must be concurrent unless the Criminal Code expressly requires consecutive sentences for specific offences.

The totality principle guides the determination: the court may impose consecutive sentences for separate offences, particularly where there were separate victims, but must ensure the total sentence does not exceed what is just and appropriate in all the circumstances.
The court must always impose consecutive sentences for offences involving violence.
expl: Under s. 718.2(c) of the Criminal Code, the court must take into account the principle that where consecutive sentences are imposed, the combined sentence should not be unduly long or harsh — this is the totality principle. Where multiple offences involve separate victims or distinct criminal acts, consecutive sentences may be appropriate to reflect the seriousness of each offence. However, the totality principle requires the court to review the total sentence and ensure it does not exceed what is a just and proportionate overall sentence for the offender's total culpability. On these facts, with two separate victims in the same robbery event, the court may consider consecutive or concurrent sentences, guided by the totality principle.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718.2(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Consecutive vs Concurrent Sentences — Totality Principle

text:
Ryan was held in pre-trial detention for eight months before being convicted and sentenced to 18 months imprisonment. Defence counsel submits that Ryan is entitled to credit for his pre-trial detention at a rate of 1.5 days credit for every 1 day of pre-sentence custody.

Under s. 719(3) and s. 719(3.1) of the Criminal Code, what is the maximum credit available for pre-sentence custody?
opts: One day of credit for each day spent in pre-sentence custody (1:1 ratio), with no enhanced credit available.
1.5 days of credit for each day of pre-sentence custody (1.5:1 ratio) in all cases.

One day of credit for each day in pre-sentence custody; the court may grant enhanced credit of up to 1.5:1 if circumstances justify it, but not if the accused was denied bail due to their criminal record or breach of bail.
Two days of credit for each day in pre-sentence custody (2:1 ratio), reflecting the harsher conditions of pre-trial detention.
expl: Sections 719(3) and 719(3.1) of the Criminal Code (as amended by the Truth in Sentencing Act, 2009) govern pre-sentence custody credit. The default credit under s. 719(3) is one day for each day of pre-sentence custody. The court may award enhanced credit of up to 1.5 days per day of pre-sentence custody under s. 719(3.1) if circumstances justify it — typically where the accused is held in remand conditions that do not allow earned remission. However, s. 719(3.1) expressly prohibits enhanced credit where the accused was denied bail primarily because of their criminal record or because they were a danger to the public under s. 515(10)(b). On those facts, only the 1:1 base credit applies.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Pre-Sentence Detention Credit — s. 719(3)

text:
Calvin, an Indigenous offender, spent 10 months in pre-sentence custody. He was unable to access Indigenous-specific programming while in remand due to the lack of programs available in the remand facility. At sentencing, defence submits that the harsh conditions of remand — including isolation and absence of culturally appropriate supports — should result in enhanced credit beyond 1.5:1.

Under the Criminal Code and established sentencing principles, may a sentencing judge award pre-sentence custody credit at a ratio greater than 1.5:1 to account for particularly harsh remand conditions including absence of cultural programming?
opts: Yes — where remand conditions were particularly harsh, including the absence of Indigenous culturally appropriate programming, the court may award credit in excess of 1.5:1.
No — s. 719(3.1) imposes an absolute cap of 1.5:1; no credit in excess of that ratio may be awarded regardless of remand conditions.
Yes — but only if the Crown admits the conditions were inadequate.
Yes — but only if the accused was held in administrative segregation for the entire period.
expl: Section 719(3.1) of the Criminal Code imposes a strict cap: the court shall not give credit for pre-sentence custody at a rate greater than 1.5 days for each day spent in custody. This is a hard statutory cap that cannot be exceeded by the sentencing judge regardless of how harsh the conditions of remand were. The argument that culturally inappropriate or otherwise harsh remand conditions should attract extra credit beyond 1.5:1 has been rejected by courts applying the statutory cap. The Gladue and systemic disadvantage factors are relevant to the overall fitness of the sentence, but they do not override the s. 719(3.1) statutory ceiling. Defence may instead argue those conditions as a mitigating factor that reduces the overall sentence.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 719(3.1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Pre-Sentence Detention Credit — Statutory Cap

text:
Sophie is a 24-year-old university student with no prior criminal record who pleads guilty to mischief under $5,000. The sentencing judge is considering whether to grant a discharge. The Crown is neutral; defence submits that a discharge is in the best interests of the accused and not contrary to the public interest.

Under s. 730 of the Criminal Code, which of the following conditions must be met before a court may grant an absolute or conditional discharge?
opts: The accused must consent to the discharge and the Crown must not object.
The accused must be a first offender under the age of 18.

The offence must not be one for which a minimum punishment is prescribed and the maximum punishment must not be 14 years or more; the court must be satisfied that a discharge is in the best interests of the accused and not contrary to the public interest.
The accused must have successfully completed a rehabilitation program before the discharge may be granted.
expl: Section 730(1) of the Criminal Code provides that where an accused is convicted of an offence (other than one punishable by a minimum term of imprisonment or by imprisonment for 14 years or more, or for life, or for life as the minimum), the court may, if it considers it to be in the best interests of the accused and not contrary to the public interest, instead of convicting the accused, direct that the accused be discharged absolutely or conditionally. The discharge may be absolute (no conditions) or conditional (subject to a probation order). A discharge results in no conviction being registered despite a finding of guilt. Crown consent and completion of a rehabilitation program are not statutory preconditions.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 730(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Absolute and Conditional Discharges — s. 730

text:
An Indigenous offender is convicted of fraud. In sentencing submissions, defence counsel presents a Gladue report documenting the offender's history of childhood trauma in the residential school system, substance abuse, and systemic disadvantage. Crown counsel argues the Gladue factors are irrelevant because the offence is serious and did not arise from Indigenous background.

Under s. 718.2(e) of the Criminal Code and R. v. Gladue, [1999] 1 SCR 688, is Crown counsel's position correct?
opts: Yes — Gladue factors apply only when the offence is directly caused by the accused's Indigenous background.
Yes — Gladue factors do not apply to serious offences; they are reserved for minor or summary conviction matters.
No — the court must consider the unique systemic or background factors affecting Indigenous offenders and alternatives to incarceration in all cases involving Indigenous offenders.
No — but only if the offender is registered under the Indian Act; urban Indigenous offenders are not entitled to a Gladue analysis.
expl: Section 718.2(e) of the Criminal Code requires courts to consider all available sanctions other than imprisonment that are reasonable in the circumstances, with particular attention to the circumstances of Aboriginal offenders. In R. v. Gladue, [1999] 1 SCR 688 and R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13, the Supreme Court held that the Gladue analysis must be applied in every case involving an Indigenous offender, regardless of the seriousness of the offence or whether the specific offence was directly linked to Indigenous background. The court must consider systemic factors and community-based alternatives. The failure to conduct a Gladue analysis for an Indigenous offender is an error in principle.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 718.2(e); R. v. Gladue, [1999] 1 SCR 688; R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Gladue Principles — s. 718.2(e)

text:
Parliament enacted a mandatory minimum sentence of five years imprisonment for a drug trafficking offence. A sentencing judge finds that in the particular case before the court, a sentence of five years would be grossly disproportionate to the offender's moral blameworthiness. The judge considers whether to strike down the mandatory minimum as a violation of s. 12 of the Charter.

Under s. 12 of the Charter and the test in R. v. Nur, 2015 SCC 15, what is the test for determining whether a mandatory minimum sentence violates the prohibition against cruel and unusual treatment or punishment?
opts: A mandatory minimum violates s. 12 if it results in a sentence that is greater than the offender's moral blameworthiness in any case.

A mandatory minimum violates s. 12 if the sentence it requires is grossly disproportionate to what is fit and appropriate, either in the case of the offender before the court or in a reasonable hypothetical case covered by the provision.
A mandatory minimum violates s. 12 only if the judge proves the specific offender suffered psychological harm from the sentence.
Mandatory minimum sentences are constitutional by definition because Parliament has authority to set minimum penalties; s. 12 cannot be used to strike down statutory minimums.
expl: In R. v. Nur, 2015 SCC 15, and R. v. Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13, the Supreme Court confirmed that a mandatory minimum sentence violates s. 12 of the Charter if it requires the imposition of a sentence that is grossly disproportionate — a sentence so excessive as to outrage standards of decency. The court may find a s. 12 violation in two ways: (1) by examining the sentence required in the specific case before the court; or (2) by considering whether the provision requires a grossly disproportionate sentence in a reasonably foreseeable hypothetical case that the law could apply to. If a mandatory minimum would be grossly disproportionate in a reasonable hypothetical case, the provision may be struck down as unconstitutional even if the specific offender's sentence is not itself grossly disproportionate.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 12; R. v. Nur, 2015 SCC 15; R. v. Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Mandatory Minimum Sentences — s. 12 Charter

text:
Alejandro is convicted of theft under $5,000. He has no income, is living in a shelter, and has significant mental health issues. He asks the sentencing judge to waive the victim surcharge. The Crown insists the surcharge is mandatory.

Under s. 737 of the Criminal Code as currently in force, may the sentencing judge waive the victim surcharge?
opts: No — the victim surcharge is mandatory and cannot be waived under any circumstances.
Yes — the judge may waive the surcharge if the offender establishes undue hardship.
Yes — the judge always has an unlimited discretion to waive the surcharge.
No — but the judge may defer payment of the surcharge for up to 10 years.
expl: The victim surcharge under s. 737 of the Criminal Code has had a complex legislative history. Under the 2019 amendments (Bill C-75), the court was given discretion to waive the surcharge where payment would cause the offender undue hardship. Prior to the 2019 amendments, the surcharge was mandatory with no waiver power (as enacted by the Increasing Offenders' Accountability for Victims Act, 2013). The Supreme Court in R. v. Boudreault, 2018 SCC 58 struck down the mandatory surcharge as unconstitutional under ss. 7 and 12 of the Charter for offenders in Alejandro's circumstances. Following Boudreault, Parliament reintroduced a waiver discretion in s. 737(3): the court may order that no surcharge be paid if it is satisfied that the offender is unable to pay it or that paying it would cause undue hardship to the offender or their dependants.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 737(3); R. v. Boudreault, 2018 SCC 58
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Victim Surcharge — s. 737

text:
A 15-year-old pleads guilty to mischief causing damage of $3,000 to a community centre. The youth justice court judge wishes to involve the victim, parents, community members, and youth workers in developing an appropriate plan for the young person before sentencing.

Under s. 19 of the YCJA, which of the following best describes the conferencing mechanism the judge may use?
opts: Conferencing under the YCJA is available only as an extra-judicial measure and cannot be ordered once a young person has pleaded guilty.

A youth justice court judge may convene or cause to be convened a conference for the purpose of obtaining advice on the appropriate youth sentence, including advice from the victim, parents, and community members.
Conferencing is mandatory whenever a young person pleads guilty to an offence causing property damage exceeding $1,000.
Conferencing requires the written consent of Crown counsel before the judge may order it.
expl: Section 19 of the YCJA authorizes a youth justice court judge, a justice, a youth worker, or other persons to convene or cause to be convened a conference for the purpose of making decisions under the Act. A conference may be convened at any stage of proceedings — before or after a finding of guilt — and may be used to obtain advice on: appropriate extra-judicial measures, conditions for judicial interim release, sentence, and review of youth sentences. A conference may include the young person, the young person's parents or guardians, the victim, members of the extended family, community members, and youth justice professionals. It is a restorative justice mechanism consistent with the YCJA's rehabilitative philosophy. Participation is not mandatory for any party, and conferences do not replace the court's sentencing function.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 19
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Conferencing — YCJA s. 19

text:
A 16-year-old is charged with aggravated assault. A local newspaper reporter attends the youth court proceedings and wishes to publish a story identifying the young person by name, including a photo. The young person has not been sentenced to an adult sentence.

Under s. 110 of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the default rule regarding publication of identifying information about a young person who is the subject of youth court proceedings?
opts: Publication of identifying information about a young person is prohibited unless the young person consents and the court orders publication.

Publication of identifying information about a young person who has not received an adult sentence is prohibited; however, the young person may themselves apply to the court for an order permitting publication.
Publication of identifying information is prohibited as a default rule, but becomes automatically permitted once the young person turns 18.
There is no prohibition on publication because youth court proceedings are open to the public.
expl: Section 110(1) of the YCJA prohibits any person from publishing the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a person dealt with under the Act. This prohibition applies to young persons who have not received an adult sentence. However, s. 110(2) and (3) create limited exceptions. Notably, s. 110(2)(b) provides that a young person who is 18 years of age or older may apply to the youth justice court for an order permitting publication. Also, publication may be permitted if a young person is at large and dangerous (s. 110(4)), or in certain other exceptional circumstances. The publication ban is a default rule reflecting the YCJA's principle of protecting young persons' privacy to facilitate rehabilitation.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 110(1), s. 110(2)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110

text:
The accused is acquitted by a jury of first degree murder after the trial judge incorrectly directed the jury on the law of mens rea. The Crown wishes to appeal the acquittal.

Under s. 676 of the Criminal Code, on what grounds may the Crown appeal an acquittal by a jury?
opts: The Crown may appeal on any ground — questions of law, fact, or mixed fact and law — as of right.
The Crown may appeal only on a question of law, as of right.
The Crown may not appeal a jury acquittal in Canada because of the constitutional prohibition against double jeopardy.
The Crown may appeal on any ground with leave of the Court of Appeal, including questions of fact.
expl: Section 676(1) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General or counsel acting on behalf of the Attorney General may appeal against a verdict of acquittal or a judgment in favour of the accused on any ground of appeal that involves a question of law alone. The Crown's right to appeal an acquittal is significantly more restricted than the accused's appeal rights. The Crown may appeal a jury acquittal only on a question of law (including a legal error in the trial judge's charge to the jury), and this is an as-of-right appeal. The Crown cannot appeal on questions of fact, mixed fact and law, or on the ground that the verdict was unreasonable. If the Crown's appeal is successful, the Court of Appeal may set aside the acquittal and enter a conviction, order a new trial, or make any other order that justice requires (s. 686(4)).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1), s. 686(4); Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(h)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Crown Appeal of Acquittal — s. 676

text:
The accused pleads guilty to sexual assault and is sentenced to a conditional sentence order of 18 months in the community. The Crown, believing the sentence is demonstrably unfit, seeks to appeal the sentence to the Court of Appeal.

Under s. 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code, on what basis may the Crown appeal a sentence imposed at trial?
opts: The Crown may appeal a sentence only if the trial judge committed an error of law in the sentencing principles applied.
The Crown may appeal a sentence with leave of the Court of Appeal, on the ground that the sentence is inadequate.
The Crown may appeal a sentence as of right, without leave, whenever the Crown considers the sentence manifestly inadequate.
The Crown has no right to appeal a sentence imposed following a guilty plea.
expl: Section 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General may appeal to the Court of Appeal against the sentence passed by a trial court, with leave of the Court of Appeal, unless the sentence is one fixed by law. On a Crown sentence appeal, the Court of Appeal applies the standard of review from R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64: the Court of Appeal may intervene only if the sentence is demonstrably unfit or the trial judge committed an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence. The ground for a Crown sentence appeal is that the sentence is 'inadequate' from the Crown's perspective — essentially, demonstrably unfit on the low end. Leave is required for Crown sentence appeals, unlike the Crown's as-of-right appeal on questions of law from acquittals (s.676(1)(a)).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1)(d); R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Crown Sentence Appeal — s. 676(1)(d)

text:
The accused was acquitted of impaired driving. The trial judge, after finding the accused's blood alcohol level to be 0.092 at the time of testing, concluded that this was insufficient to establish impairment at the time of driving.
