The Ontario FRO may transmit the support order to the BC Family Maintenance Enforcement Program (FMEP) for enforcement in that province. The recipient does not need to retain BC counsel or attend BC court. Similarly, support obligations may be varied by courts in either province through the ISO Act process. This scheme prevents payors from escaping their obligations by relocating to another province.
caseRef: Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Inter-Provincial Enforcement — ISO Act and RESA

text:
Darius owes $12,000 in child support arrears. The FRO has already suspended his driver's licence, but he continues to drive using a vehicle registered in his corporation's name. The FRO is considering additional enforcement tools. A colleague tells Darius that because the vehicle is in the corporation's name, the FRO cannot suspend the vehicle registration.

Does the FRSAEA provide for suspension of vehicle registrations (licence plates), and does a corporate registration insulate the vehicle?
opts: The FRSAEA authorizes suspension only of the payor's personal driver's licence, not vehicle registrations; corporate registrations are fully exempt.

Under FRSAEA s.35, the Director may also direct the Registrar to refuse to validate or to suspend a vehicle permit (licence plate) held by the payor; however, a vehicle registered in the name of a corporation that the payor controls may be scrutinized, and courts have looked past corporate registration in egregious cases.
Vehicle registration suspension is automatic once a driver's licence is suspended; no additional FRO step is required.
The FRO has no authority over vehicle registrations in any circumstances.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.35 provides that the Director may direct the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to refuse to validate or to suspend a vehicle permit (commonly known as a licence plate) held by the payor. This is a separate enforcement mechanism from driver's licence suspension under s.34. The suspension of vehicle permits may target vehicles registered in the payor's personal name. Where a vehicle is registered in a corporation's name, the effectiveness of this mechanism depends on whether the court or Director can pierce the corporate veil — an issue that arises in egregious cases of enforcement avoidance. Simply registering a vehicle in a corporation the payor controls does not automatically insulate it from enforcement scrutiny.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 35
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Vehicle Permit Suspension — FRSAEA s.35

text:
Donna and Raymond separated in January 2021. They attempted reconciliation for several months, failing permanently in October 2021. Raymond never commenced divorce proceedings. In March 2026, Donna files an application for equalization of net family property under the Family Law Act. Raymond argues her claim is statute-barred under the FLA's specific limitation period.

What limitation period governs Donna's equalization claim under the Family Law Act, and when does it begin to run?
opts: Donna's equalization claim is subject to the general two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of separation in January 2021; her claim filed in March 2026 is statute-barred.

Under FLA s.7(3), a claim for equalization of net family property must be commenced within two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity, within two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into, or within six years after the day the spouses separate and there is no reasonable prospect of resumption of cohabitation; Donna's claim must be analyzed under these specific triggering events.
There is no limitation period for equalization claims in Ontario; they may be brought at any time during or after the marriage.
The limitation period for equalization is ten years from separation.
expl: Family Law Act s.7(3) provides three specific limitation periods for equalization claims: (a) two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity; (b) two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into; or (c) six years after the day the spouses separate in circumstances that appear to be permanent. These are specific limitation periods under the FLA that displace the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. On the facts, if there is no divorce and no separation agreement, Donna must rely on the six-year period from the date of permanent separation (October 2021, when the reconciliation finally failed). If the permanent separation date is October 2021, the six-year period runs to October 2027, meaning Donna's March 2026 application is within time. Raymond's argument based on the January 2021 date may fail if the permanent separation date is found to be October 2021.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 7(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Equalization Claim Limitation — FLA s.7(3)

text:
Peter and Joan separated in 2020. Joan remained in the matrimonial home with the children. No exclusive possession order was ever sought. The home is registered in both names. Peter recently announced he plans to sell the home and wants Joan to vacate. It is now 2026. Joan consults a lawyer about whether she can now apply for exclusive possession.

Is Joan's potential application for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home subject to a limitation period, and what law governs?
opts: There is no limitation period for exclusive possession applications; Joan may apply at any time regardless of how long the parties have been separated.

The general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to exclusive possession applications, running from the date Joan knew or ought to have known her right to possession was threatened; a court would assess when the claim was discovered.
The limitation period for exclusive possession is six years from the date of separation under FLA s.7(3).
Exclusive possession orders are not available more than one year after separation.
expl: Unlike equalization claims, which have specific limitation periods under FLA s.7(3), there is no equivalent specific limitation period in the FLA for exclusive possession applications. Courts have applied the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period to exclusive possession claims. The two-year period runs from the date the claim was discovered — that is, when Joan knew or ought to have known that: (a) she had a right to apply for exclusive possession; and (b) a proceeding was an appropriate means to enforce that right. The discovery date may be the date of separation, or it may be later — such as when Peter first threatened to sell the home or demanded Joan vacate. Joan should seek advice urgently as the limitation period may apply.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Limitation Period — Exclusive Possession Applications

text:
David and Isabella cohabited in a conjugal relationship for 3.5 years. During this time, they had no children together. They now separate and Isabella seeks spousal support. David argues she does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because they were not married and she must establish an independent ground.

Under FLA s.29, does Isabella qualify as a 'spouse' for the purpose of spousal support?
opts: No — FLA s.29 requires five years of continuous cohabitation; 3.5 years is insufficient.

Yes — under FLA s.29, 'spouse' for Part III (support) purposes includes a person who has cohabited with another person in a conjugal relationship continuously for at least three years; Isabella satisfies this threshold and may seek spousal support.
No — a person who has not been married cannot be a 'spouse' under any FLA provision.
Yes — any cohabitation in a conjugal relationship, regardless of duration, qualifies under FLA s.29.
expl: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part III (support obligations) to include a person who has cohabited with another person in a conjugal relationship continuously for a period of not less than three years. Isabella and David cohabited continuously for 3.5 years in a conjugal relationship — meeting the three-year threshold. Accordingly, Isabella qualifies as a 'spouse' under s.29 and has standing to apply for spousal support under the FLA. The threshold under s.29 is three years of continuous cohabitation OR parenthood of a child together; the marriage threshold does not apply for FLA support purposes.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | FLA s.29 — Definition of Spouse for Support

text:
Angela and Robert cohabited for 18 years. Throughout the relationship, they each worked full-time, pooled all income into a joint account, made joint decisions about property purchases, cared for their two children together, and treated their financial lives as fully merged. Robert's investments grew significantly. On separation, Angela claims a proportionate share of all accumulated wealth.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, which of the four joint family venture factors are most clearly present on these facts, and what remedy does a finding of joint family venture support?
opts: None of the four joint family venture factors are present because Angela and Robert both worked; the JFV doctrine only applies where one party worked exclusively in the home.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the four JFV factors are: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family; all four appear strongly present on these facts (pooled income, joint decisions, children, mutual full commitment); a finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy rather than a quantum meruit calculation.
The joint family venture doctrine entitles Angela to exactly 50% of all assets regardless of her actual contributions.
Joint family venture applies only where both parties have signed a formal partnership agreement.
expl: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court identified four factors indicating a joint family venture: (1) mutual effort — working together toward common goals; (2) economic integration — pooling resources and finances; (3) actual intent — the parties' own understanding of their relationship as a joint enterprise; and (4) priority of the family — decisions made in the interests of the family unit. On the facts, all four factors are strongly present: 18 years of cohabitation, pooled income, joint property decisions, raising children together, and fully merged financial lives. A finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy for the unjust enrichment claim — Angela would receive a share proportional to her actual contribution to the jointly acquired wealth, which may be close to 50% given the full economic integration.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Joint Family Venture — Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors

text:
During their common-law relationship, Craig transferred $80,000 from his personal savings account to an investment account he registered solely in his partner Leigh's name, without any written explanation. Three years later, they separate. Craig claims the money was always his and that Leigh holds it on a resulting trust for him. Leigh argues it was a gift to her.

What is the presumption of resulting trust and how does it apply to Craig's $80,000 transfer to Leigh?
opts: In a common-law relationship, any transfer of money between partners is presumed to be a gift; Craig bears the burden of proving otherwise.

Under the law of resulting trusts, where one person gratuitously transfers property to another person with whom they are not married, there is a presumption of resulting trust — the recipient holds the property on trust for the transferor; Craig does not need to prove the money was a loan; Leigh bears the burden of rebutting the presumption by proving Craig intended a gift.
The presumption of resulting trust applies only to transfers of real property, not to money transfers.
The presumption of resulting trust applies only between spouses, not between common-law partners.
expl: The presumption of resulting trust arises at common law when a person gratuitously transfers property to another person who is not their spouse. In such cases, the law presumes the recipient holds the property on a resulting trust for the transferor — the transferor's intent is presumed to be to retain beneficial ownership. The recipient bears the burden of rebutting this presumption by showing the transferor intended an outright gift. Note that for married spouses, the presumption of advancement applies in the opposite direction (a gratuitous transfer between spouses is presumed a gift). Craig and Leigh are not married, so the resulting trust presumption applies to the $80,000 transfer; Leigh must prove Craig intended a gift to rebut it.
caseRef: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfers

text:
After a 14-year common-law relationship, Nadia and Vincent separate. During the relationship, Nadia quit her job to look after their three children and Vincent's parents while Vincent built his contracting business from scratch. The home is registered in Vincent's name. Nadia claims unjust enrichment. Vincent argues he paid Nadia a weekly allowance of $200 for her household work, constituting a juristic reason for retaining the benefit.

Under the unjust enrichment doctrine as articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, does the payment of a modest allowance constitute a juristic reason negating Nadia's claim?
opts: Any payment to the claimant, however modest, constitutes a complete juristic reason for the defendant's retention of the benefit.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the juristic reasons category includes: existing contracts, disposition of law, donative intent, and other policy-based reasons; a nominal $200/week payment may not fully reflect the value of Nadia's contributions and would not constitute a complete juristic reason if the payment was grossly disproportionate to the benefit conferred; the court assesses the reasonableness of the juristic reason and the degree to which it corresponds to the enrichment.
The juristic reason analysis is not applicable to common-law relationships; unjust enrichment is assessed purely on the value of the claimant's labour.
A modest payment always provides a complete juristic reason because parties are free to contract on any terms they choose.
expl: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court confirmed that the absence of a juristic reason is an essential element of unjust enrichment. Juristic reasons include: (1) a contract; (2) a disposition of law; (3) donative intent (the claimant intended to gift the benefit); and (4) other policy reasons. A nominal payment of $200/week as an 'allowance' for extensive child-rearing and household management over 14 years may not constitute an adequate juristic reason if it is grossly disproportionate to the value of the services rendered. The court would assess whether the allowance corresponds meaningfully to the enrichment conferred. Where a payment captures only a fraction of the economic value of the contributions, it does not negate the unjust enrichment claim in full — the claim may succeed for the excess enrichment beyond the payment.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Unjust Enrichment — Juristic Reason Analysis

text:
The federal Securities Act creates a national securities regulator and makes insider trading a federal offence. Quebec enacts its own Securities Act that also prohibits insider trading and imposes provincial administrative penalties. A Quebec trader is investigated under both statutes for the same trades. Quebec argues its law is entirely valid under s. 92(13) (property and civil rights). The federal government argues the provincial law conflicts with the federal scheme.

Under the double aspect doctrine, when can both federal and provincial laws on the same subject matter be simultaneously valid?
opts: Both laws can never be simultaneously valid when they regulate the same conduct; one must yield to the other through pith and substance analysis.
Both laws can coexist when they are identical; conflicting provisions cannot both be valid under the double aspect doctrine.

Under the double aspect doctrine, a single subject of legislation may be validly addressed by both federal and provincial legislatures where each law is directed at a different constitutional purpose and is valid in pith and substance under its respective head of power, even if both target the same conduct.
The double aspect doctrine applies only to criminal law matters under s. 91(27); it has no application to securities regulation.
expl: The double aspect doctrine recognizes that some matters have both a federal and a provincial dimension and may be lawfully regulated by both levels of government simultaneously. The doctrine is founded on the principle that characterization of legislation by pith and substance may place it under a federal head of power while the same conduct may also be validly regulated provincially under a different head of power from a different constitutional perspective. Both laws may coexist as long as each is valid in its own right; if operational conflict or frustration of federal purpose arises, federal paramountcy resolves the conflict by rendering the provincial law inoperative to the extent of the inconsistency.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Double Aspect Doctrine — Same Matter, Dual Validity

text:
First National Bank of Canada, a federally chartered bank, operates a branch in Ontario. Ontario attempts to apply a provincial consumer protection statute requiring all lenders to disclose certain information and to comply with a maximum interest disclosure standard. The bank argues that as a federal undertaking under s. 91(15) (banking), it is immune from provincial consumer protection legislation under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity.

Under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity as restated in Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, does a provincial consumer protection statute apply to a federally chartered bank?
opts: No. All provincial laws are automatically inapplicable to federally chartered banks because banking is an enumerated federal head of power under s. 91(15).

Yes. Under Canadian Western Bank, interjurisdictional immunity requires impairment of the vital or essential core of banking; general consumer protection disclosure requirements do not impair that core and may validly apply to federally chartered banks.
No. Provincial consumer protection laws are always inapplicable to banks because they regulate the terms of banking contracts, which is the exclusive domain of federal banking regulation.
Yes, but only if the provincial law has been specifically authorized by the federal Bank Act.
expl: In Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22, the Supreme Court of Canada rejected the broad application of interjurisdictional immunity and held that the doctrine should be applied with restraint, reserved for impairment of the vital and essential core of a federal undertaking. Provincial consumer protection laws that impose general disclosure obligations do not impair the essential banking functions of a federally chartered bank; they regulate the bank's conduct in the consumer marketplace in a manner similar to how any commercial lender would be regulated. Such laws are not inapplicable under IJI. If a conflict arises with specific federal banking regulation, the paramountcy doctrine would resolve it.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91(15) (banking), s. 92(13); Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Interjurisdictional Immunity — Banks and Indian Reserves

text:
Ontario enacts legislation requiring all handguns sold in the province to undergo a mandatory waiting period of 10 days before transfer to the purchaser. The federal government challenges the legislation, arguing it relates to criminal law under s. 91(27) of the Constitution Act, 1867. Ontario responds that the law is directed at public safety and consumer protection under s. 92(13) (property and civil rights) and s. 92(16) (matters of a merely local or private nature).

In determining the constitutional validity of Ontario's waiting period law, what is the first step in the division of powers analysis?
opts: Determine whether the law conflicts with existing federal legislation under the federal paramountcy doctrine.
Determine the pith and substance of the law — its dominant purpose and legal effect — to identify which head of power it falls under.
Determine whether the law engages s. 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Determine whether the law falls under interjurisdictional immunity by assessing whether it affects a vital or essential part of a federal undertaking.
expl: The pith and substance analysis is the foundational first step in any division of powers analysis. The court identifies the dominant purpose and legal effect of the legislation to characterize it as falling under a federal (s. 91) or provincial (s. 92) head of power. Incidental effects on the other level's jurisdiction do not render a law unconstitutional so long as its dominant character is within the enacting government's jurisdiction. Only after characterization does the court assess paramountcy, interjurisdictional immunity, or Charter compliance.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Pith and Substance Analysis

text:
Ontario enacts the Environmental Protection and Responsibility Act, which imposes civil liability on manufacturers of toxic chemicals for environmental damage caused by their products. The dominant purpose of the legislation is to create a compensation scheme for environmental harm caused by the chemical industry in Ontario. The legislation has the incidental effect of raising the cost of manufacturing chemicals in Ontario, which may affect interprovincial trade. A chemical manufacturer argues the law is really trade regulation within exclusive federal jurisdiction.

Under the pith and substance doctrine, when do incidental effects of provincial legislation on a federal head of power render the provincial law unconstitutional?
opts: Any incidental effect of provincial legislation on a matter within federal jurisdiction automatically renders the provincial law unconstitutional.
Incidental effects on federal matters render the provincial law unconstitutional only if the federal government objects in writing.

Under the pith and substance doctrine, incidental effects of a provincial law on matters within federal jurisdiction do not render the law unconstitutional, provided the dominant purpose and legal effect of the law fall within a valid head of provincial power; a law is characterized by its pith and substance, not by its incidental effects.
A provincial law with any effect on interprovincial trade is automatically within federal jurisdiction under the trade and commerce power in s. 91(2).
expl: The pith and substance doctrine holds that legislation is characterized by its dominant purpose and legal effect (its 'matter'), not by its incidental effects on other matters. A valid provincial law may incidentally affect matters within federal jurisdiction without becoming unconstitutional on that basis alone, as long as its dominant purpose is within provincial jurisdiction. Here, a provincial compensation scheme for environmental harm is properly characterized as a matter of property and civil rights (s. 92(13)) and environmental protection, which are provincial. The incidental effect of raising production costs, while touching on interprovincial trade, does not change the pith and substance of the legislation.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Pith and Substance Analysis — Incidental Effects

text:
Parliament enacts legislation prohibiting certain pesticides that scientific evidence indicates may cause harm to human health. The legislation creates criminal offences and monetary penalties for manufacturers, distributors, and retailers who produce or sell the prohibited pesticides. Alberta argues the legislation is really environmental regulation within provincial jurisdiction and that Parliament cannot use the criminal law power as a pretext to regulate industries that fall under provincial jurisdiction.

Under s. 91(27) of the Constitution Act, 1867, what are the constitutional requirements for a valid exercise of the federal criminal law power?
opts: Federal criminal law must target conduct that was already criminal at common law; Parliament cannot criminalize new conduct using s. 91(27).

Federal criminal law under s. 91(27) requires: (1) a prohibition; (2) a penalty; and (3) a criminal law purpose — typically protection of a public interest such as public safety, public health, public morality, or security. The breadth of the criminal law power is wide, and courts do not require Parliament to prove the prohibited conduct is immoral.
Section 91(27) authorizes Parliament to regulate any industry if it attaches a criminal penalty to the regulatory scheme; the penalty alone satisfies the criminal law purpose requirement.
Parliament may only use s. 91(27) to legislate on matters exclusively federal in nature; the criminal law power cannot be used for matters that also fall within provincial jurisdiction.
expl: The three-part test for valid federal criminal law was established in Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783, and confirmed in R v Morgentaler, [1993] 3 SCR 463. The legislation must: (1) contain a prohibition; (2) backed by a penalty; and (3) be directed at a criminal law purpose such as public safety, public health, public morality, public order, or security. Courts give Parliament wide latitude under s. 91(27); protection of public health — including from harmful pesticides — is a recognized criminal law purpose. The law is not invalidated simply because it also incidentally affects provincially regulated industries, provided the dominant purpose is genuinely criminal.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91(27); Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783; R v Morgentaler, [1993] 3 SCR 463
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Section 91(27) — Breadth of Federal Criminal Law Power

text:
Ontario enacts legislation regulating insurance contracts, including requiring insurance companies operating in Ontario to include mandatory coverage provisions and prohibiting certain exclusions. An insurance company incorporated federally under the Insurance Companies Act argues that the provincial legislation is constitutionally invalid because insurance is a federally regulated industry and therefore falls outside provincial jurisdiction.

Under s. 92(13) of the Constitution Act, 1867, can Ontario validly regulate the terms of insurance contracts entered into in Ontario?
opts: No. Insurance is an enumerated head of federal power under s. 91 of the Constitution Act, 1867, and provinces have no jurisdiction to regulate it.

Yes. Section 92(13) grants the provinces exclusive jurisdiction over 'property and civil rights in the province,' which has been held to include the regulation of contracts, including insurance contracts. Provinces may validly regulate the terms of insurance contracts so long as the dominant purpose of the provincial law is not in pith and substance within a federal head of power.
No. Federally incorporated insurance companies are immune from provincial regulation under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity.
Yes, but only if the Ontario legislation is identical to the federal Insurance Companies Act provisions.
expl: Section 92(13) of the Constitution Act, 1867, grants provinces exclusive jurisdiction over 'property and civil rights in the province.' This head of power has been interpreted broadly to include the law of contracts, torts, and property, as well as the regulation of many commercial activities including insurance. Provincial regulation of the terms of insurance contracts — mandatory coverage provisions, prohibited exclusions — falls within s. 92(13) as a matter of property and civil rights. Insurance is not an enumerated federal head of power; federal jurisdiction over insurance companies (their incorporation and solvency) derives from s. 91(2) (trade and commerce) and related powers, but the regulation of insurance contracts in the province remains predominantly provincial.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 92(13); Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Section 92(13) — Property and Civil Rights in the Province

text:
Police officers execute a lawful arrest warrant for a man named John Smith. When they arrive at the suspect's door, the suspect says he is not John Smith but is in fact James Brown. The police nonetheless arrest him and tell him only 'you're being arrested.' They do not inform him of the specific offence for which he is being arrested or why they believe he is the person named in the warrant. He is held for six hours before the misidentification is discovered. He argues his s. 10(a) rights were violated.

Under s. 10(a) of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, what information must police provide to a person upon arrest or detention?
opts: Police must read the person the complete text of the charging document; nothing less satisfies s. 10(a).
Section 10(a) requires only that police inform the person that they are being arrested; the specific grounds need not be disclosed until the person is formally charged.

Under s. 10(a) of the Charter, police must promptly inform the person of the reasons for their arrest or detention — including the specific offence alleged and the factual basis for the arrest — with sufficient detail to allow the person to understand why they are being deprived of their liberty and to exercise their right to retain counsel meaningfully.
Section 10(a) applies only to detention, not to arrest; a person who is formally arrested has no s. 10(a) right to be informed of reasons.
expl: Under s. 10(a) of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, everyone has the right on arrest or detention to be informed promptly of the reasons therefore. This means police must inform the arrestee of the specific offence alleged and sufficient factual basis to understand why they are being deprived of liberty. As established in R v Evans, [1991] 1 SCR 869, the obligation requires meaningful communication of the grounds — not a formal reading of charges — but sufficient to enable the person to understand the reason for detention and to instruct counsel. Where a person is arrested based on a warrant and the arresting officer fails to explain the reason, s. 10(a) is breached.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(a); R v Evans, [1991] 1 SCR 869
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 10(a) — Right to Be Informed of Reasons for Arrest or Detention

text:
A federal statute imposes a mandatory minimum sentence of three years for a first-time offender convicted of simple possession of a small quantity of a controlled substance. A first-time offender convicted of possessing a small personal-use quantity receives a three-year sentence. The offender argues the mandatory minimum is grossly disproportionate to the offence and constitutes cruel and unusual punishment under s. 12 of the Charter.

What is the legal test under s. 12 of the Charter for determining whether a mandatory minimum sentence constitutes cruel and unusual punishment?
opts: A sentence is cruel and unusual only if it is physically painful or degrading; a prison term, however long, cannot constitute cruel and unusual treatment.
A mandatory minimum sentence violates s. 12 if the judge believes a shorter sentence would have been more appropriate in the circumstances.

Under s. 12 of the Charter, a sentence — including a mandatory minimum — constitutes cruel and unusual punishment if it is grossly disproportionate to what is appropriate in the circumstances; the court considers whether the sentence is so excessive as to outrage standards of decency, which includes considering not only the particular offender but reasonably foreseeable applications of the mandatory minimum.
A mandatory minimum sentence is automatically constitutional because Parliament has determined it is a necessary deterrent.
expl: Under s. 12 of the Charter and as confirmed in R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15, and R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13, a sentence constitutes cruel and unusual punishment if it is grossly disproportionate — so excessive as to outrage standards of decency. Courts assess gross disproportionality by considering the particular offender and also the reasonably foreseeable application of the mandatory minimum to other offenders (the 'reasonable hypothetical' approach). A mandatory minimum that would impose a grossly disproportionate sentence on a reasonable hypothetical offender at the low end of the offending spectrum — such as a first-time possession offender — violates s. 12.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 12; R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15; R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 12 — Cruel and Unusual Treatment or Punishment

text:
A provincial government relies on a notwithstanding clause declaration under s. 33 to override s. 15 of the Charter in connection with a statute that excludes women from certain senior public service positions. A claimant argues that regardless of the s. 33 override, s. 28 of the Charter independently guarantees Charter rights equally to male and female persons and cannot be displaced by s. 33.

What is the legal effect of s. 28 of the Charter, and does a s. 33 notwithstanding clause override negate the protection afforded by s. 28?
opts: Section 28 is merely a declaration of principle with no independent legal force; it adds nothing to the Charter beyond what s. 15 already provides.

Section 28 guarantees that the rights and freedoms in the Charter are guaranteed equally to male and female persons; however, s. 28 does not create freestanding independent rights — it operates as an interpretive guarantee that existing Charter rights apply equally regardless of sex.
Section 28 creates an absolute bar against any law that distinguishes between men and women, overriding all other Charter provisions including s. 1 and s. 33.
Section 28 applies only to provincial governments; the federal government is not bound by the guarantee of equal protection for male and female persons.
expl: Section 28 of the Charter provides that the rights and freedoms referred to in the Charter are guaranteed equally to male and female persons. Section 28 operates as an interpretive guarantee applied to rights enumerated elsewhere in the Charter; it ensures that Charter rights are interpreted and applied equally without discrimination based on sex. Section 28 does not create freestanding rights independent of the rest of the Charter, but it does reinforce the equal application of Charter rights. Its relationship with s. 33 is contested in academic literature, but the prevailing view is that s. 28 operates as a guide to interpretation of enumerated rights rather than as a separate override on s. 33.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 28
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 28 — Charter Rights Guaranteed Equally to Male and Female Persons

text:
A provincial legislature enacts a statute declaring that it operates notwithstanding s. 2 (fundamental freedoms) and ss. 7 to 15 of the Charter. The legislation requires provincial civil servants to remove face coverings while providing or receiving public services. A civil servant challenges the law, arguing the notwithstanding clause declaration does not cover the Charter provision she relies on.

Under s. 33 of the Charter, which Charter rights may a legislature override using a notwithstanding clause declaration, and for how long does such a declaration remain in effect?
opts: A legislature may override any Charter right, including democratic rights (ss. 3-5), mobility rights (s. 6), and language rights (ss. 16-23), through a valid notwithstanding clause declaration.

Under s. 33, a legislature may expressly declare that a statute operates notwithstanding s. 2 or ss. 7 to 15 of the Charter only; democratic rights, mobility rights, minority language rights, and equality rights under s. 28 cannot be overridden. A valid declaration ceases to have effect after five years but may be re-enacted.
A notwithstanding clause declaration overrides all Charter rights for ten years, after which it must be renewed.
Section 33 applies only to federal legislation; provincial legislatures cannot invoke the notwithstanding clause.
expl: Section 33 of the Charter permits Parliament or a provincial legislature to expressly declare that an Act or a provision thereof shall operate notwithstanding a provision included in s. 2 or ss. 7 to 15 of the Charter. The notwithstanding clause cannot override democratic rights (ss. 3, 4, 5), mobility rights (s. 6), official language rights (ss. 16–23), minority language education rights (s. 23), or the equality guarantee in s. 28. A declaration under s. 33 ceases to have effect five years after it comes into force but may be re-enacted for successive five-year periods.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 33
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 33 — Notwithstanding Clause — Scope and Use

text:
Police officers on foot patrol stop a young Black man walking in a high-crime neighbourhood late at night. The officers question him, demand his identification, and ask him to empty his pockets. The officers do not tell him he is free to leave. The man complies and contraband is found. He argues the police interaction constituted a psychological detention without reasonable cause, violating s. 9 of the Charter.

Under R v Grant, when does a police-citizen interaction constitute a psychological detention within the meaning of s. 9 of the Charter?
opts: A detention under s. 9 requires physical restraint; a verbal interaction that does not involve physical contact can never constitute a detention.
Any police questioning of a citizen automatically constitutes a detention that must be based on reasonable grounds.

Under R v Grant, a psychological detention occurs when a reasonable person in the circumstances would conclude that they were not free to walk away; relevant factors include the nature of the police conduct, the setting, and whether the person was singled out in circumstances that would lead them to feel compelled to comply.
A psychological detention is only established where the police officer explicitly says the person is not free to leave.
expl: In R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court of Canada established a contextual test for psychological detention under s. 9. A person is psychologically detained — even without physical restraint — when a reasonable person in the particular circumstances would conclude they were not free to go. The court considers the nature of the police conduct (tone, form of demands vs. requests), the setting, and whether the encounter is targeted at that individual in a way that would lead a reasonable person to feel compelled to comply. On these facts, the combination of demands, the absence of any indication the person could leave, and the targeted nature of the stop may constitute a psychological detention.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 9; R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 9 — Psychological Detention and the Grant Test

text:
The Ontario Environmental Compliance Approval Board is granted a statutory power to approve or refuse applications for environmental licences. The Board Chair, facing a backlog, issues a directive assigning all routine approvals to a junior staff officer with no statutory appointment to the Board. The staff officer approves several applications. A third party argues the approvals are invalid because the statutory power was not lawfully exercised.

Under the principle of delegatus non potest delegare, can the Board Chair lawfully subdelegate the Board's statutory power of decision to a non-Board staff officer?
opts: Yes. The Chair has broad administrative authority to organize the Board's work and may assign decision-making to staff as an administrative matter.

No. Under the principle delegatus non potest delegare, a person or body to whom a statutory power of decision has been delegated cannot further subdelegate that power to another person unless the enabling statute expressly authorizes subdelegation.
Yes. Where a tribunal is facing a significant backlog, administrative necessity allows subdelegation to qualified staff without explicit statutory authorization.
No. The Board Chair may never delegate any functions; all decisions must be made by the full Board sitting together.
expl: The maxim delegatus non potest delegare (a delegate cannot further delegate) is a foundational principle of administrative law in Canada. Where Parliament or a legislature grants a statutory power of decision to a specific body or official, that body or official must exercise the power personally and cannot further subdelegate it to another person or body unless the enabling statute expressly authorizes subdelegation. A staff officer who is not a statutory member of the Board has no authority to exercise the Board's statutory power of decision; approvals issued by the staff officer are therefore ultra vires and invalid, regardless of the backlog or administrative convenience.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22; Interpretation Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. I.11; delegatus non potest delegare principle
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Delegation and Subdelegation — Delegatus Non Potest Delegare

text:
An Ontario securities regulatory body has a structure in which its staff conduct investigations and make prosecution recommendations to a panel of commissioners. The same commissioners who receive the staff's recommendation also sit as adjudicators at the hearing on the merits. A respondent argues that the institution suffers from structural bias because the adjudicators who will decide the case have been exposed to the staff's investigative conclusions before the hearing.

Under Canadian administrative law, when does overlap between investigative and adjudicative functions within the same institution give rise to a reasonable apprehension of institutional (structural) bias?
opts: Any overlap between investigative and adjudicative functions within the same institution automatically gives rise to a reasonable apprehension of bias.
Institutional bias arises only where the same individual personally conducted the investigation and also sits as adjudicator; there is no concern where different individuals handle each function.

Under IWA v Consolidated-Bathurst Packaging Ltd, [1990] 1 SCR 282, and the reasonable apprehension of bias test from Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, a reasonable apprehension of institutional bias arises where the institutional structure is such that the adjudicators have been exposed to one-sided, partisan information about the case before the hearing — as where the adjudicating panel receives and considers the staff's investigative recommendations. The test remains whether a reasonable and informed person, knowing all the circumstances, would apprehend a risk of unfairness.
Institutional bias is not a recognized ground of challenge in Canadian administrative law; bias challenges must be directed at individual adjudicators, not the institution.
expl: Under Canadian administrative law, the reasonable apprehension of bias test from Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, applies to both individual and institutional bias. Where the structure of a regulatory body is such that the adjudicators who will decide a case have been exposed to the investigating staff's one-sided conclusions before the hearing, a reasonable and informed person may apprehend that the adjudicators have been infected by the investigative perspective, impairing their ability to approach the hearing with an open mind. Courts apply a contextual analysis considering the degree of overlap and how the pre-hearing exposure occurred, rather than applying an automatic rule.
caseRef: Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369; IWA v Consolidated-Bathurst Packaging Ltd, [1990] 1 SCR 282
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Institutional Bias — Overlapping Investigative and Adjudicative Functions

text:
A licensed nurse practitioner has for 15 years renewed her practice permit annually without any hearing or conditions. The nursing regulatory college has never required a hearing for straightforward renewals and has a published policy stating that renewal applications 'will be approved unless there are identified conduct concerns.' The college now imposes new mandatory conditions on the nurse's permit renewal without providing any hearing or opportunity to respond to concerns. The nurse argues she had a legitimate expectation of renewal without conditions.

Under the Baker framework and Canadian administrative law, what does the doctrine of legitimate expectations entitle a claimant to when a public authority departs from a past practice or representation?
opts: Legitimate expectations entitles the claimant to both the promised procedure and the substantive outcome — the authority must grant the same outcome it previously provided.

In Canadian administrative law, the doctrine of legitimate expectations is limited to procedural entitlements; it does not require the authority to grant the same substantive outcome but does require the authority to follow promised or previously observed procedures — here, providing an opportunity to be heard — before departing from past practice.
Legitimate expectations has no application where the public authority publishes a policy; the existence of a written policy extinguishes any legitimate expectation arising from past conduct.
Legitimate expectations applies only where the claimant received an individualized written guarantee from a senior official; a general published policy cannot give rise to legitimate expectations.
expl: Under Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that in Canadian administrative law the doctrine of legitimate expectations generates procedural rights only — it does not compel a particular substantive outcome. Where a public authority has, through its conduct or representations, led a party to expect that a particular process will be followed, the party is entitled to that process before the authority departs from the expected course. The published policy stating renewals 'will be approved unless there are conduct concerns,' combined with 15 years of practice, creates a legitimate expectation of a hearing or an opportunity to respond before conditions are imposed; it does not, however, guarantee renewal without conditions.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Reference re Canada Assistance Plan (BC), [1991] 2 SCR 525
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Legitimate Expectations — Procedural vs Substantive in Canadian Law

text:
A federal parole board panel denies an offender early release. The parole board is not governed by the Ontario SPPA (being a federal body). Its enabling statute is silent on whether reasons for decisions must be provided. The offender argues that the common law duty of procedural fairness requires reasons. The Crown argues that without a statutory requirement, reasons are not owed.

Under the Baker framework, does the common law duty of procedural fairness impose an obligation to give reasons for an administrative decision where the enabling statute is silent on the matter?
opts: No. Where an enabling statute is silent on reasons, no duty to give reasons exists; reasons are exclusively a statutory requirement.
Yes. Baker established an absolute duty to give written reasons for every administrative decision in Canada, irrespective of the statutory framework.

Under Baker v Canada, the duty to provide reasons is part of the content of procedural fairness that is determined contextually by weighing the five Baker factors; where the decision has serious consequences for the individual, the process resembles adjudication, and the affected party has no other means to understand the decision, the common law may require reasons even without a statutory mandate.
The duty to give reasons in the absence of a statute applies only where there is a Charter breach in the proceedings.
expl: In Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that a duty to give reasons may form part of the common law duty of procedural fairness, independent of any statutory requirement. The duty arises from a contextual analysis of the Baker factors — particularly the significance of the decision to the individual and the adjudicative nature of the process. Parole decisions deprive individuals of their liberty interest in early release and have serious personal consequences; the adjudicative nature of parole hearings and the significance of the outcome together support a common law duty to provide reasons, even though the federal parole board is not subject to the Ontario SPPA.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Duty to Give Reasons — Common Law Obligation Outside SPPA

text:
A party before an Ontario professional licensing tribunal loses at first instance. The enabling statute provides a statutory right of appeal to the Divisional Court on any question of law or fact. Instead of filing an appeal, the party immediately applies for judicial review in the Divisional Court, arguing the tribunal's decision was unreasonable. The tribunal argues judicial review should be dismissed because the statutory appeal is an adequate alternative remedy.

Under Ontario law, when does the existence of a statutory right of appeal bar judicial review?
opts: A statutory right of appeal never bars judicial review; parties always have a choice between judicial review and statutory appeal.
A statutory right of appeal always bars judicial review; the applicant must pursue all statutory appeals before judicial review is available.

Under Ontario administrative law, where a statute provides an adequate alternative remedy — such as a statutory right of appeal — courts generally decline to exercise their discretion to grant judicial review, unless special circumstances exist, such as the tribunal lacking jurisdiction, the appeal mechanism being inadequate or inaccessible, or there being a constitutional question not addressed by the appeal.
Judicial review is barred only where the statutory appeal was actually filed and abandoned; failing to file an appeal at all does not engage the alternative remedy doctrine.
expl: Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2, and consistent with the common law, courts retain a discretion to decline to grant judicial review where an adequate alternative remedy exists, including a statutory right of appeal. The existence of a right of appeal to the Divisional Court on any question of law or fact is a strong reason to decline judicial review in favour of the statutory appeal mechanism, which is designed to address the exact issues raised. Courts will nonetheless grant judicial review in special circumstances — such as where the appeal mechanism is inadequate (e.g., limited to questions of law only), where there is a jurisdictional issue, or where the statutory appeal cannot address a Charter or constitutional issue. Here, the statutory appeal covers all questions of law and fact, making it an adequate alternative remedy.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Border Services Agency) v CB Powell Limited, 2010 FCA 61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Adequate Alternative Remedy — Bar to Judicial Review

text:
An applicant receives an adverse decision from an Ontario tribunal on March 1, 2024. She does not file a judicial review application until September 15, 2024 — more than six months later. The Judicial Review Procedure Act provides that an application for judicial review shall be made promptly and, in any event, within 30 days after the impugned decision unless the court extends the time. The applicant argues the six-month delay should be excused because she was unaware of the time limit.

Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, what is the time limit for commencing a judicial review application in Ontario, and what is required for an extension of time?
opts: There is no time limit for commencing judicial review in Ontario; an applicant may bring an application at any time as long as the issue is still justiciable.

Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, an application for judicial review must be made promptly and in any event within 30 days after the impugned decision; the court has discretion to extend the time and will consider factors including the length of the delay, the reasons for delay, prejudice to the respondent, and the merits of the underlying application.
The time limit for judicial review in Ontario is six months; the applicant has filed within the applicable time limit.
The 30-day limit is absolute and jurisdictional; no extension is possible under any circumstances.
expl: Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 5, an application for judicial review shall be made promptly and, in any event, within 30 days after the date of the decision, unless the court extends the time. Courts exercise their discretion to extend the time by considering the length of the delay, whether there is a reasonable explanation for it, whether granting the extension would prejudice the respondent, and whether the underlying application has arguable merit. Ignorance of the time limit is relevant but not automatically sufficient; the applicant here has significantly exceeded the 30-day period and will need to provide a compelling explanation for a six-month delay.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 5
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Delay in Bringing Judicial Review — Time Limits

text:
A refugee claimant challenges a removal order through judicial review. While the application is pending, the claimant voluntarily departs Canada. The government moves to dismiss the application as moot, arguing there is no longer a live controversy. The claimant argues the court should still exercise its discretion to decide the question because of its systemic importance to other refugees facing removal orders.

Under the Borowski test for mootness, when should a court exercise its discretion to decide a case even where the immediate controversy no longer exists?
opts: A court must always dismiss a moot case; it has no discretion to proceed once the immediate controversy is resolved.
A court should always decide cases of systemic importance, regardless of mootness.

Under Borowski v Canada (AG), [1989] 1 SCR 342, the mootness analysis proceeds in two stages: (1) determine whether the required tangible and concrete dispute has disappeared; and (2) if so, exercise discretion to consider whether to decide the case anyway, weighing factors such as: whether the parties retain an adversarial relationship, whether judicial economy supports a decision on a systemic issue, and whether the court would be departing from its proper role in deciding the question.
A case is moot only if both parties agree that there is no longer any live controversy; unilateral departure does not make a case moot.
expl: The leading Canadian authority on mootness is Borowski v Canada (Attorney General), [1989] 1 SCR 342, where the Supreme Court established a two-stage test. Stage one asks whether the required adversarial relationship and live controversy still exists. If the matter is moot (stage one), stage two asks whether the court should nonetheless exercise its discretion to decide the case, weighing: (a) whether an adversarial context still exists to sharpen the issues; (b) whether judicial economy supports addressing a systemic issue to avoid future litigation; and (c) whether the court would be overstepping its institutional role by deciding a hypothetical issue. Systemic importance to other refugees is a factor but not automatically sufficient to justify a decision in a moot case.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Borowski v Canada (Attorney General), [1989] 1 SCR 342
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Mootness on Judicial Review — Borowski Test

text:
An applicant seeks judicial review of an Ontario tribunal decision. She argues the tribunal considered communications between tribunal staff and government officials that were not part of the formal hearing record. She wants the reviewing court to consider a stack of emails her counsel obtained through an access to information request, which were not before the tribunal. She argues these emails demonstrate bad faith and improper external influence.

On judicial review, what material is ordinarily before the reviewing court, and when may a court consider evidence outside the formal tribunal record?
opts: The reviewing court must consider all relevant evidence, including evidence obtained after the decision was made, because judicial review is a de novo proceeding.

The reviewing court is generally confined to the certified tribunal record — the material that was before the decision-maker — but may receive additional evidence in limited circumstances, such as to establish procedural unfairness, jurisdictional facts, or a reasonable apprehension of bias that could not be demonstrated from the record alone.
The reviewing court is absolutely confined to the tribunal record; no additional evidence from any source may ever be admitted.
Because judicial review is a supervisory jurisdiction, the court may call live witnesses and conduct its own fact-finding on the merits.
expl: On judicial review, the reviewing court is ordinarily confined to the record that was before the decision-maker — the certified tribunal record. This principle reflects the supervisory (not appellate de novo) nature of judicial review: the court examines the decision-maker's process and reasoning on the basis of the evidence that was before them, not new evidence. However, courts have recognized narrow exceptions where additional evidence may be admitted: for example, to demonstrate a breach of natural justice or procedural fairness (such as bias or a denial of the right to be heard) that is not apparent from the record, or to establish jurisdictional facts that go to the court's or the tribunal's jurisdiction. The emails in this case, going to alleged bad faith or improper external influence, may fall within the procedural fairness exception.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Certified Tribunal Record — Scope of Review

text:
A court on judicial review finds that a federal immigration tribunal's decision to refuse a refugee claim was unreasonable — the tribunal's reasoning was internally incoherent and failed to address key evidence. The applicant asks the court to substitute a decision granting refugee protection, arguing that only one outcome is possible on the evidence. The government argues the court should remit the matter to a differently constituted tribunal.

What is the ordinary remedy when a reviewing court finds that an administrative decision is unreasonable under the Vavilov framework, and when, if ever, may a court substitute its own decision?
opts: When a decision is found unreasonable, the court must always substitute its own correct decision without remitting to the tribunal.
When a decision is found unreasonable, the court must always remit to the tribunal; it can never substitute its own decision.

Under Vavilov, when a court finds a decision unreasonable, the ordinary remedy is remittal — sending the matter back to the decision-maker for redetermination — because the decision-maker, not the court, has the statutory authority to make the decision on the merits. The court may substitute its own decision only in the rare case where the circumstances make it unnecessary or unfair to remit, such as where only one outcome is legally possible.
The court always has a choice between remittal and substitution; the choice is made based on whichever is more convenient.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, when a reviewing court finds a decision unreasonable, the ordinary remedy is to remit the matter to the decision-maker for redetermination, with or without directions. This reflects the constitutional principle that it is the statutory decision-maker — not the court — that has the authority to make the decision. The court may, however, substitute its own decision in exceptional circumstances where there is only one possible outcome, remittal would serve no useful purpose, or fairness to the parties demands finality. Mere inefficiency or delay in the tribunal system is not a sufficient reason to substitute judgment.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | JR Remedies — Remittal vs Substitution of Decision

text:
A national environmental advocacy organization seeks judicial review of a federal Cabinet decision approving a pipeline project. The organization has no direct economic interest in the pipeline. It argues the approval violates federal environmental assessment legislation. The government argues the organization lacks standing to bring the application because it is not directly affected by the decision.

Under Canadian law, what is the test for public interest standing to seek judicial review, and can a public interest organization without a direct personal stake bring a judicial review application?
opts: No. Only persons directly and personally affected by a decision have standing to seek judicial review; public interest organizations can never obtain standing.
Yes, automatically. Any organization with an interest in good government has standing to challenge any government decision.

Under the Canadian Doctors for Refugee Care v Canada (Attorney General), 2015 FC 904, and Canadian Council of Churches v Canada, [1992] 1 SCR 236, public interest standing may be granted where: (1) there is a serious justiciable issue; (2) the applicant has a real stake or genuine interest in the issue; and (3) the proposed proceeding is a reasonable and effective means of bringing the issue to court. Courts have discretion and will consider whether other parties with standing are willing to challenge the decision.
Yes. Any federally incorporated non-profit organization has automatic standing to challenge federal government decisions.
expl: Under Canadian Council of Churches v Canada (Minister of Employment and Immigration), [1992] 1 SCR 236, and the subsequent public interest standing trilogy refined in Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence Society, 2012 SCC 45, the Supreme Court of Canada established a three-part test for public interest standing: (1) there is a serious justiciable issue; (2) the applicant is directly affected by or has a genuine stake in the issue; and (3) the proposed proceeding is a reasonable and effective means of bringing the issue before the court — including consideration of whether others with standing are available. Public interest standing is discretionary and not automatic; a lack of personal economic stake does not preclude standing where the other criteria are met.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canadian Council of Churches v Canada (Minister of Employment and Immigration), [1992] 1 SCR 236; Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence Society, 2012 SCC 45
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Public Interest Standing on Judicial Review

text:
A licensed electrician receives a notice from the Electrical Safety Authority stating that his licence is suspended pending investigation of a complaint. The electrician argues the SPPA applies and that he is entitled to a hearing before his licence is suspended. The Authority argues the SPPA does not apply because the suspension is a temporary administrative step, not a final decision.

Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, what is the threshold requirement for the SPPA to apply to a tribunal's decision?
opts: The SPPA applies to any decision made by any government body in Ontario, including purely administrative and ministerial decisions.
The SPPA applies only to decisions of courts; administrative tribunals are governed exclusively by their enabling statutes.

Under s. 3(1) of the SPPA, the Act applies to a proceeding by a tribunal in the exercise of a 'statutory power of decision' conferred by or under an Act of the Legislature where the tribunal is required by or under such Act or otherwise by law to hold or to afford parties an opportunity for a hearing.
The SPPA applies only where the decision-maker is explicitly named in a Schedule to the SPPA.
expl: The Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3(1), provides that the Act applies to a proceeding by a tribunal in the exercise of a statutory power of decision where the tribunal is required by or under the Act or otherwise by law to hold or afford the parties to the proceeding an opportunity for a hearing before making a decision. The definition of 'statutory power of decision' in s. 1 means a power or right conferred by or under a statute to make a decision deciding or prescribing the legal rights, powers, privileges, immunities, duties or liabilities of any person or party. A licence suspension is a statutory power of decision and the SPPA applies if a hearing is required.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 1 (definitions), s. 3(1) (application)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | SPPA Application — When Does the SPPA Apply

text:
At a hearing before an Ontario professional discipline tribunal, the regulator seeks to introduce communications obtained by its compliance officers during a covert inspection that was conducted without notice or consent. The respondent argues the communications were obtained through an improper inspection process and that the evidence should be excluded. The tribunal chair states that under s. 15 of the SPPA, all relevant evidence is admissible and the tribunal has no power to exclude it.

Does s. 15 of the SPPA require a tribunal to admit all relevant evidence regardless of how it was obtained, or does the tribunal retain discretion to exclude evidence in appropriate circumstances?
opts: Yes. Section 15(1) of the SPPA requires admission of all relevant evidence; tribunals have no discretion to exclude evidence regardless of how it was obtained.

No. Under s. 15(2) of the SPPA, a tribunal may exclude evidence where its admission would be unduly prejudicial or where its exclusion is required by law, including considerations of fairness; the SPPA does not mandate admission of all evidence in all circumstances.
Yes, but only privileged communications — such as solicitor-client communications — may be excluded by the tribunal; all other relevant evidence must be admitted.
No. The tribunal may exclude any evidence it finds unpersuasive without giving reasons; reliability is the only factor.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1), a tribunal may receive as evidence any oral testimony and any document or thing relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding, whether or not it would be admissible in a court of law. However, s. 15(2) recognizes that tribunals may be required by law to exclude evidence — for example, where the exclusion is required to protect solicitor-client privilege, informer privilege, or other legally protected categories. Additionally, tribunals retain common law discretion under the duty of fairness to consider whether admitting improperly obtained evidence would make the proceeding fundamentally unfair. The chair's absolute statement that 'all relevant evidence is admissible' overstates the SPPA's reach.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1), s. 15(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Exclusion of Evidence — SPPA Section 15(2)

text:
An Ontario professional regulatory tribunal issues a final written decision on April 1 suspending a dentist's licence for three months. On April 5, the tribunal's chair notices a clerical error in the written decision: the effective date of the suspension was incorrectly stated as 'April 30' rather than 'May 1' as was intended and orally pronounced. The tribunal reconvenes on its own initiative and issues a corrected decision. The dentist argues the tribunal was functus officio after its original decision and had no authority to issue any corrected decision.

Under the doctrine of functus officio and SPPA s. 21.2, when may an Ontario tribunal correct or reconsider its own final decision?
opts: A tribunal is absolutely functus officio once it issues a final written decision; it may not change any aspect of the decision under any circumstances.
A tribunal may reconsider any aspect of its decision at any time as long as the proceeding has not been appealed or judicially reviewed.

Under the doctrine of functus officio, a tribunal generally loses jurisdiction over a matter once it has issued a final decision; however, it retains a limited common law authority to correct clerical or accidental errors in a final decision without reconsidering the merits, and s. 21.2 of the SPPA provides a broader statutory basis for reconsideration where the tribunal's rules permit.
A tribunal may never correct a written decision; errors in written decisions can only be corrected by a reviewing court on judicial review.
expl: The doctrine of functus officio holds that once a tribunal has issued a final decision on the merits, it is generally divested of jurisdiction over the matter and cannot reconsider its decision. However, the common law has long recognized a narrow exception for the correction of clerical errors — where the written decision does not accurately record what was decided — without re-opening the merits. Additionally, the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2, provides a statutory mechanism for reconsideration where the tribunal's rules permit. Correcting a transcription error in the effective date (which does not change the substance of the decision) falls within the clerical error exception and is permissible; it does not re-open the merits of the suspension.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2; Chandler v Alberta Association of Architects, [1989] 2 SCR 848
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Reconsideration — SPPA Section 21.2 and Functus Officio

text:
A respondent in a professional discipline hearing governed by the SPPA wishes to compel a reluctant witness to attend and give evidence. The witness refuses to attend voluntarily. The respondent's counsel asks the tribunal to issue a summons requiring the witness to appear and produce documents. The tribunal clerk states the tribunal has no power to compel witnesses and that only courts can issue summonses.

Under s. 12 of the SPPA, does an Ontario tribunal governed by the Act have the power to issue summonses requiring witnesses to attend and produce documents at a hearing?
opts: No. Only superior courts have the power to issue summonses and require witness attendance; administrative tribunals cannot compel witnesses.

Yes. Under s. 12 of the SPPA, a tribunal may require by summons the attendance of any person and the production at the hearing of any document or thing that the tribunal considers relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding.
Yes, but only where the witness is a government employee; private individuals cannot be summonsed by an administrative tribunal.
Yes, but only where the enabling statute of the tribunal expressly grants this power; the SPPA alone is insufficient.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 12(1), a tribunal may by summons require the attendance of any person at a hearing and the production of any document or other thing if the tribunal considers it relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding. A summons under s. 12 is not restricted to government employees; it applies to any person. The SPPA provides this power independently of the tribunal's enabling statute. Failure to comply with a tribunal summons may be enforced through superior court contempt proceedings under s. 13 of the SPPA.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 12(1), s. 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Summons to Witness — SPPA Section 12

text:
A person submits a FIPPA request to the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services for records relating to a foster care placement decision that affected her family. The records contain personal information about the biological parents of the child placed in care. The Ministry refuses access under s. 21 (personal privacy exemption). The requester argues that disclosure is necessary to allow her to understand the basis for a decision that directly affected her.

Under FIPPA, is s. 21 (personal privacy) a mandatory or discretionary exemption, and does it have any exceptions that might allow disclosure?
opts: Section 21 is a discretionary exemption; the Ministry may choose to disclose personal information in any case where it considers disclosure in the public interest.

Section 21 is a mandatory exemption — the Ministry shall refuse to disclose personal information about a third party; however, FIPPA provides defined exceptions including where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny, or where the individual to whom the information relates has consented.
Section 21 is a mandatory exemption with no exceptions; personal information about a third party can never be disclosed under FIPPA.
Section 21 is not an exemption at all; it simply requires the Ministry to notify the third party before making a disclosure decision.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1), an institution shall refuse to disclose personal information to any person other than the individual to whom the information relates — making it a mandatory exemption (using 'shall'). However, s. 21(1) is subject to a number of defined exceptions, including: where the person to whom the information relates has consented (s. 21(1)(a)); where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny (s. 21(1)(f)(iii)); and other listed exceptions. The institution must consider whether any exception applies before invoking the mandatory exemption.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA Exemptions — Section 21 Personal Privacy

text:
A journalist submits a FIPPA request to the Ontario Ministry of Infrastructure for all emails and text messages sent on the personal cell phones of three senior Ministry officials relating to a major infrastructure contract. The Ministry responds that personal cell phones are not Ministry property and that personal device communications are outside its custody and control. The journalist argues the communications relate to Ministry business and should be disclosed.

Under FIPPA, when are records held on personal devices of government officials in the custody or control of the institution for the purpose of the right of access?
opts: Records are in the custody or control of the institution only if they are physically stored on government servers or equipment; personal device communications can never be subject to FIPPA access requests.

Under FIPPA, 'custody or control' is determined by examining the nature of the record's content and the purpose for which it was created; records on personal devices that relate to the conduct of government business may be in the control of the institution for FIPPA purposes, even if they are not physically stored on government equipment.
All communications on personal devices by government officials are automatically subject to FIPPA regardless of their content.
FIPPA applies only to records created during working hours on government premises; personal device communications outside office hours are never subject to FIPPA.
